text: The PVV is talking about budget caps. These are indeed an enormously perverse price incentive that we must eradicate from the healthcare market. If we put forward a proposal, we can therefore cooperate on it, and the PVV will vote in favor. It is the same story: a majority will be achieved in the House, and we will finally be rid of them. After all, we previously passed a motion to ban budget caps, but it was not implemented. That is why you are calling on the Minister to do so.

But the information is all very precise. The wolf can already be shot. That is already possible. In case of emergency, the police may give the order — or they may do it themselves — to shoot a wolf. That has already happened. So the truth must be told. Moreover, people also have a duty to protect animals. That is not happening adequately. We will discuss that shortly. The point is the following. We continuously asked for intervention when it was still time, and at that time, no one, absolutely no one, supported us. I therefore have difficulty accepting that our party in particular comes under fire, while we warned about this misery. We are not going to cooperate with that anymore. If they are all allowed in, you don't shoot them all. And that is exactly what is going to happen. Just watch.

No. Once again: we do not have 76 seats, and I cannot implement my election manifesto as long as I do not have an absolute majority. Perhaps it is better, for any party whatsoever, that no single party holds an absolute majority. You will therefore always have to make compromises. We have done that too. So yes, we were unable to secure everything. That is simply the way it is, and my voters understand that very well as well. What we did secure is the strictest asylum policy ever, and that is truly number one, also with our voters. But you also see—as I just mentioned—that of those billions, approximately 4 billion net tax relief goes to citizens and just over 1 billion net to businesses. That is a ratio of 4 to 1. Is it bad that those businesses receive tax relief? No, not at all! People work there. It is entirely justified that they receive tax relief as well. A portion of that tax relief also goes to small and medium-sized enterprises. The grocer and the baker on the corner benefit from the rollback of the SME profit exemption. Think of the energy tax, too. Those aren't large corporations or Maoist issues. Those are just normal businesses benefiting from it: small and medium-sized enterprises in the Netherlands. I am proud. The PVV also stands for small and medium-sized enterprises. Yes, okay, there is something to be said for the dividend tax as well — I agree with you on that — but on balance, about 1 billion goes to companies and 4 billion to citizens. It won't get any more PVV than this.

Thank you, Chair. Today we are discussing the problems in Ter Apel. There is much to be said about this. For years, Ter Apel has been terrorized by asylum thugs. Asylum seekers walk around with knives. There are acts of vandalism and burglaries. Shoplifting occurs constantly. Violence is even committed against security guards, general practitioners, and staff working in the asylum seeker center. Just last Friday, two underage asylum seekers from Ter Apel harassed girls on the train and assaulted two NS employees at Roosendaal station. It is not just in Ter Apel that things are getting out of hand. In other municipalities with an asylum seeker center, such as Emmen or Budel, there is also misery caused by asylum seekers. In Maarheeze, NS even threatened to close the train station. People in Budel no longer even dare to go to the supermarket because they know that groups of asylum seekers are walking around with knives in their pockets. Chair. This is, of course, unacceptable. It is unacceptable that our own people no longer even dare to go out on the streets because they are being terrorized by asylum seekers. The cause of all this misery is, of course, the massive influx of asylum seekers in recent years. Under previous cabinets, more than 450,000 asylum seekers were admitted. This is not a reception crisis, but an influx crisis and a nuisance crisis. It is the Minister's task to clean up this mess. That is no easy task, especially not when you are constantly being obstructed. As far as the PVV is concerned, we will quickly implement the measures agreed upon in the framework agreement to limit the influx. This also applies to the agreements to tackle nuisance. A pilot project has now been launched in Ter Apel to address the nuisance. Asylum seekers from safe countries causing a nuisance must report twice a day. I understand that the Minister is bound by laws and court rulings, but a reporting obligation alone is not enough for us. As far as the PVV is concerned, criminal and disruptive asylum seekers must be locked up. I will therefore submit a motion with the other coalition parties to detain asylum seekers causing a nuisance. In conclusion, Chair. For the PVV, it is not only important that the Cabinet works on reducing the influx and the nuisance, but also that the Cabinet focuses on return, preferably forced. Criminal and disruptive asylum seekers and those from safe countries must be quickly put on a plane to their countries of origin. How will the Minister ensure this? Thank you.

We see that public transport is becoming increasingly expensive. The PVV has also argued on several occasions for doing something about this. We will have some good suggestions for this during the debate later. It was not without reason that the PVV was of the opinion that NS could cease all its ancillary activities so that money could be saved, so that the money could have gone to the traveler. You know, Mr. El Abassi, I say through you, Chair: the best advertisement for public transport is still a train that runs on time, a train that is clean, and a train in which it is safe. That is the very best advertisement to get more people to take the train, to generate more revenue, and ultimately to be able to lower fares.

Chair. Time and again, the House expresses its concerns regarding private equity and the encroaching commercialization of healthcare. But what is the Minister doing? In her letter of March 5, she simply disregards the adopted motions. No ban, but a vague, targeted approach and a look ahead to the Healthcare Business Conduct Act (Wibz). We will just have to wait and see what happens. But the PVV parliamentary group continues to oppose commercial healthcare chains and private equity without reservation, because these parties have only one goal: to make excessive profits at the expense of sick people, and doing so with our healthcare funds. Take Co-Med, for example. The quality of care deteriorated, and when it went bankrupt, 50,000 people were left without a GP. Primary care is already under enormous pressure. Practices are full and are not accepting new patients. How many people are still waiting for GP registration after the Co-Med debacle? Has everyone who was still owed money by Co-Med received that money in the meantime? The Minister admits that the collapse of such large healthcare organizations jeopardizes the continuity of care, but repeatedly shifts the responsibility onto health insurers under the pretext that they have a duty of care and must arrange this. Research by SiRM and Finance Ideas demonstrates that profit distributions attract new providers, but when the focus lies on money—and that is where the focus lies—the quality and accessibility of care suffer. Private clinics and independent treatment centers (ZBCs) focus on low-complexity, profitable care. They compete with regular hospitals and lure scarce healthcare personnel with higher wages and better working hours. Does the Minister agree with the PVV parliamentary group that this exacerbates the staff shortage in regular healthcare? And yes, Chair, there are certainly privately funded start-ups with good intentions that stimulate healthcare innovations, but does that outweigh the sky-high dividend payouts? Does that outweigh public money disappearing into private pockets, I ask the Minister. Our healthcare system is not a business model for the greedy. Yet VWS seems to be a paradise for those looking to get rich quick at the expense of those in need of care and the taxpayer. Independent treatment centers and even the largest ambulance service, the Witte Kruis, spin off profitable activities into holding companies and work with subcontractors. The NZa can only scrutinize the healthcare BVs, not the structures surrounding them.

For the time being, not very many sheep—only 3% to 5%—are kept behind wolf-proof fences, which are often inadequate or insufficiently sound. We therefore want those teams to be deployed. This has already been stated by various colleagues. Animal husbandry regulations must also be adhered to. Animals must be protected against attacks by predators. So help people with this, including hobby animal keepers. They are asking for it, and they want it. Naturally, this applies to farmers as well. I will briefly run through this. I will spare you a lot of nonsense, as Mr. Eerdmans puts it. The PVV brought the lowering of protected status to a majority back in 2023. At that point, the police could already make decisions on the spot, as could a mayor. I would like to say to Mr. Van Campen: go and talk to the mayor of Westerveld. I did that myself. He is now the former mayor. I even sat with him in the House here. It is a slightly different story than how it appeared in the media, so go and talk to him. Because if I am attacked with a pitchfork, my rabbit will bite too. We are therefore consistently and persistently sticking to sterilizing the existing packs. I would appreciate a response from the State Secretary. I have a few more questions. I would like to request national policy regarding preventive measures that have by now been proven effective, including contraceptive procedures: castration, sterilization, and that hormone dart. I would like to ask for use to be made of scientific insights and experiences from abroad, where they do not know all those problems and that panic. In that regard, I am indeed also thinking of the wolf fence teams; I will use the Dutch term. The correct placement of those fences must also be monitored. I would like to request a tightening of controls on the proper application of those wolf-deterrent measures, improvements, and protective equipment for the benefit of grazing animals. We are standing up for that as well. In any case, I would like to request — and this applies to all animals — strict enforcement of the duty of care for kept animals, as established in Article 1.6 of the Animal Keepers Decree. So go ahead and do that. I also just mentioned tackling poaching, because poaching is still taking place on a massive scale in the Netherlands. We need to do something about that. Therefore, more personnel really need to be deployed, as well as teams from the forestry service and the animal police working together, well and heavily armed. We all need to start doing that.

Thank you, Chair. In this debate, we have heard many opinions regarding the right to demonstrate and the supposed carte blanche this would grant to exempt oneself from the law. Many colleagues referred to the ECHR in this regard. Article 11.1 thereof guarantees the right to demonstrate, but Article 11.2 simultaneously states that this right may be restricted by law, for example in the interest of public safety or to prevent disorder and criminal offences. This was confirmed once again by the ruling issued by the European Court of Human Rights on January 16th last. I hope the Minister can promise to look closely at this ruling, as the response so far leaves much to be desired. The PVV is therefore not confident that he will tackle these disruptive demonstrations. Hence the following motion. The House, having heard the deliberations, noting that during the recent Extinction Rebellion "demonstration" on 11 January 2025, participants violated the law by blocking the highway, failing to carry identification, and refusing to obey police orders; noting that the Public Prosecution Service is not initiating prosecution for this despite the 700 arrests, because, in its view, blocking the highway is "a minor criminal offense"; noting that these disruptive and nuisance-causing "demonstrations" not only cost society exorbitant amounts of money and scarce police capacity, but are also life-threatening, as ambulances and other emergency services are forced to take detours; noting that no fines were issued for failing to show identification, nor for blocking the highway, nor for failing to obey police orders; requests the Minister to call the Public Prosecution Service to order and to have at least fines issued for all future violations of the law, and proceeds to the order of the day.

The motion regarding document no. 1043 receives the House's judgment. I can promise to explore with DJI whether bringing available former detention boats back into service is feasible. The motion regarding document no. 1044 also receives the House's judgment, if I may interpret the request as an investigation into the possibilities.

Well, that is what we are doing. We are halving the deductible. I think Cindy is very happy with that. The next step is abolishing the deductible. This is a first step. So I just wanted to point that out. We are, of course, cleaning up the mess from all those previous years. You raised the VAT and brought it to 21%. We didn't do that. You did so many things. You raised the deductible. We are all now reversing what the PvdA let slip. Patience is a virtue.

No, on the contrary, our signature is worth a great deal. After all, it also appears under what I just quoted from the framework agreement: climate policy must be sustainable, feasible, and implementable. It is that simple. It is up to the Minister to come up with alternative policy. We must then look at that very seriously. We must talk and negotiate about it. Are we going to do that, or are we not? If it turns out that it is not feasible, sustainable, or implementable, then we must indeed ask ourselves very seriously whether we are on the right track. Should we really keep charging ahead by focusing solely on those climate goals every time without looking at feasibility? Should we keep coming up with new climate policy time and again, or should we make room and reconsider? Shouldn't we then ask ourselves whether we are on the right track? The PVV thinks the latter. It is that simple.

I am glad that Mr. Eerdmans is talking about the root, because the root of the problem is still the influx. So I can drop that one more time as well. Right, it is both-and. I completely agree with you. These people need to return as soon as possible. You ask: where is that first plane taking them back to wherever? I completely agree with you on that. It is already the case that more people are being deported. The number leaving the Netherlands is increasing. I have a list of that. I received it this morning and I was very pleased with that list. On it, you can see that in 2023, 10,690 people left the Netherlands. In 2024, that number was 11,730. And in January 2025, 920 people have already left the Netherlands. So the number is rising. I can also distribute this list, if you would like or are interested in it. So things are definitely happening. As soon as I have legal grounds to return people to their country of origin, I will be the last to refuse. I am at the forefront of doing so. I am also holding intensive talks with the Repatriation and Departure Service. Hard work is being done to achieve this. And it is happening. There are people leaving the Netherlands, including people from safe countries, such as Morocco. Cooperation with Morocco is steadily improving, and Morocco is increasingly willing to take people back. That return process is gaining momentum. So it is not the case that nothing is happening. It is happening. However, the asylum process is being frustrated. By that, I mean that people are stacking procedures. I want to address that. I want to address this, among other things, through asylum legislation. There are people who do not want to cooperate. I want to criminalize all of that. There are people who say on the airplane steps: "I want asylum." I intend to tackle all of that, because it frustrates the entire chain. What I want to tell you is: things are definitely happening. It is a combination of factors. I am working on this, but in the meantime, I must ensure that the area around the asylum seeker centers remains liveable in the Netherlands. That is why we have now introduced the stricter approach. Of course, that is not enough. I want more. I would much rather want much more. I would also prefer to simply lock them up in prison. I would like that too. However, the problem is that I am bound. If you want to hear it: we are essentially just entangled in regulations and legislation where we have very little room. I am pushing the boundaries. I am doing my utmost to ensure this is done. That is why I also want to get that package of measures through the Houses as soon as possible.

I will try one more time. It is not on the agenda right now, but the voter wants to know. The voters voted for you. They want to know: is the PVV for or against a Nexit? Your party leader said recently, when my party leader interrupted: well, we are no longer in favor of a Nexit, because it is not feasible anyway. That is what your party leader said. What does it mean, then, that it is no longer feasible? That is something you have to fight for as a party. We are fighting for that.

No, so it is not three or four years. It is a short period that I think, if the cabinet does not fall, we will all still experience together. That is the short period I am talking about. March 2029 was the final, latest date, making the timeframe March 2027–2029. The tricky part is also that, if Mr. Claassen wants this sooner, it involves a completely different system, because then it has to be recorded, requested, tracked manually, and looked into every single time, whereas with the EHDS, for example, we handle it under the "provided that," where the patient can simply tick what they want. That is my intention with arranging the opt-out later on.

Chair, thank you. Today we are debating the amendment of the Penitentiary Principles Act, in order to limit continued criminal activity by serious criminals while in detention. Currently, within the possibilities of the law and the European Court, a number of options are on the table. For instance, two excellent amendments have passed the House that would make it considerably more difficult for prisoners to continue criminal activity. For instance, audiovisual monitoring of contact between lawyers and prisoners would be introduced. I know that a number of colleagues are concerned about the effectiveness of legal aid and such matters regarding this idea, but as far as the PVV is concerned, an excellent solution was devised for this within the amendments. The conversations would be recorded using audio and video, but—and here is the crucial point—would not be viewed or listened to. In short, in that regard, there are no concerns regarding confidentiality. So what would happen? Well, these conversations would be retained by, for example, the Netherlands Bar Association for a period of five years. Should there be reasonable suspicion within those five years that a legal aid provider is guilty of participating in a criminal organization, the conversations could be listened to after an independent commission or the Bar Association has granted permission for this. This procedure guarantees confidentiality between lawyer and prisoner and provides a certain degree of certainty for society that criminal activity by prisoners cannot be continued through communication via the lawyer: an excellent amendment and an excellent solution. The PVV is therefore disappointed in the Cabinet's stance, which is anticipating what the European Court might think of this matter. In the Cabinet's letter, I read that there is no precedent whatsoever in which the European Court of Justice has ruled on whether or not to permit the recording of conversations between prisoners and their lawyers. Furthermore, we are not talking about regular prisoners, but about prisoners who have been placed in a highly exceptional regime due to the threat posed by them and their criminal organizations. This is therefore a very specific case, in which it is to be expected that the European Court, should it come to that at all—for one must first go to court for this—will take exceptional circumstances into account and will weigh the consequences for national security and public order very heavily in deciding whether or not audiovisual recordings are permitted. This applies especially when, in principle, these recordings are not viewed or listened to as long as there is no reasonable suspicion. My question to the Cabinet is therefore: why are you so afraid of the European Court? Why is the Cabinet disregarding the democratic decision-making of the House of Representatives in this matter? This is happening under the guise of the supposed rights of convicted criminals that could stem from a possible ruling by a court that assesses everything on an individual basis and, moreover, has never ruled on such a case. Can you explain that? Mr. Chairman, thank you.

However, it is difficult to report on everything they do, because we have 98 general hospitals. So I find that a difficult task. But I have already promised to convey this to you, and I will do so. I promised you that. Then, just a brief word about Zuyderland. I had a very good conversation there. Staff shortages are significant there right now. But I am very pleased that the hospital has pledged that, should the staffing situation change due to, among other things, the changing healthcare landscape, it is prepared to shape the hospital—including the intensive care unit, emergency departments, and operating rooms—at least regarding land acquisition and signing up for the architectural plans. And during that conversation, the insurer pledged to be willing to pick up the bill of around 200 million euros. I thought that was very nice. Suppose you have enough patients in the region for two hospitals at two locations, two emergency departments, two intensive care units, and two sets of operating rooms. The difficult part is that the hospital still runs into trouble if there are staff shortages, because then the hospital simply doesn't have the personnel. So I find it very regrettable, but I will do everything I can to help the hospital cope with the staffing problems.

I don't have to do anything at all. I don't have to do anything at all, and certainly not if Ms. Yeşilgöz says so. Then not only do I not have to; then I won't do it either. But apart from that, I have indicated that we, as the PVV, do not support the boots on the ground. It is as simple as that. However, if a contribution can be made by the Netherlands later on, if it does make sense if a mission is launched and the Prime Minister were asked to provide equipment or extra funds, then of course we will do so. That Dutch person, Ms. Yeşilgöz, doesn't understand any of this at all. You say you support it, but you are simply talking right over it. That Dutch person sees 30,000 Ukrainians here in the Netherlands, who often drive expensive cars and go back to their own country on vacation. My mailbox is full of emails from people saying: they are going back to their own country! These are young people, often in their twenties or thirties, who are not going back to their own country to defend them. They are often evading conscription in their own country. Then we send our people there to let them die; I don't think so!

No, 13 million has been allocated to VluchtelingenWerk. That is the case and it will be spent. Naturally, we fully support that. I would like to discuss the additional amount further. So, it does indeed seem unwise to simply stop it if no good alternatives exist. I have indicated this as well, but it is also not wise to immediately reverse that decision without having had the chance to discuss it further at all. So, I want to wait for that, and then we will make our decision on the matter.

That was not "just now." That was a few hours ago. I have a question. I hear the honorable representative speaker of the PVV delivering a speech that I recognize. The Netherlands has changed very seriously. Immigration has disfigured our country. It is a very big problem. We no longer recognize the streetscape. We no longer feel at home. Immigration must stop. That is evident. Immigration from Africa and the Middle East must stop. But why is your party not in favor of promoting remigration? Because surely that is actually what you need? Surely that is what you should introduce? Just as people were welcomed here in an attractive way during the decades of mass immigration, through subsidized housing, through all kinds of subsidies, through an entire system of family reunification and you name it, surely you can also encourage people to return to their countries of origin? Why are you not in favor of that?

The motion regarding document no. 110 has been advised against. A patent is property and independent of policy preferences. You ask me to set conditions for the provision of public funding, but this is already happening. The further development of these principles and the toolkit is taking place under the direction of the Universities of the Netherlands and the Dutch Federation of University Medical Centres and is therefore widely supported. It has been promised that you will be informed about this in writing, which will already happen in October via a status update letter.

Thank you, Chair. I would like to begin by expressing my respect for the monarchy. Easier positions are conceivable. Fulfilling them requires a great deal of time, energy, and dedication. In the modern monarchy, this is further compounded by the sometimes precarious balance between the institution maintaining an appropriate distance from society and staying in touch with the population. The modern monarchy is increasingly subject to popularity polls. They do not lie. For example, the annual King's Day survey from last April showed that confidence in the King has fallen from 71% in 2014 to 44% in 2024. The view that the Netherlands should become a republic has risen from 15% to 25% during the same period. That is a quarter of the population. While 57% remaining support for the monarchy is still a majority, it is gradually becoming very tight. I quote a few responses to the question: what do you appreciate least about the King? Then it is mentioned: ill-considered actions demonstrating that he has limited contact with societal sentiments, no connection or feeling with what is going on among his people, a lack of involvement and concern for the real problems of the Dutch citizen, and that he receives too much money, far too much money, and on top of that, pays no taxes. In the budget we are discussing today, a salary increase of 7.18% has been included for the King. That amounts to €78,000. Among the population, there is irritation regarding the image of a King who is well-off, while the ordinary man pays at the supermarket checkout every day with tears in his eyes and wonders if he will be able to keep his home warm enough this coming winter. It would be to the King's credit to voluntarily forgo this increase, but just like in previous years, there is silence from the palace. Just as in previous years, we hoped that the King, in recognition and acknowledgment of the criticism among the population, would voluntarily forgo the salary increase as a gesture. Unfortunately, that has not happened again. Therefore, it is up to us, the House, to act. The PVV is taking up the challenge here once again. During last year's budget, we submitted a motion requesting then-Prime Minister Rutte to ask the King to forgo his salary increase of €55,000 at the time. The Prime Minister indicated that he did not wish to implement this adopted motion and disregarded it.

What Ms. Van der Plas says is incorrect. After all, I have been here in the House for eighteen years. Various debates regarding the wolf have taken place, even with this State Secretary. I wanted to make that clear. When the first female crossed the border, it was our party—which is currently under the most fire—that said: start using hormone darts immediately. We also requested this for the animals in the Oostvaardersplassen. That was not allowed. The Habitat Directive. The European Commission blocked it. They were not allowed to be driven away. They were not allowed to be sterilized. That must be said. Because the people in the gallery whom Ms. Van der Plas cites are attacking me, whereas I was the first and only one who said: watch out; we shouldn't want this in our country. That was not out of fear, but purely out of concern, also for the animal. That has faded into the background. Now it looks as if we are saying: now that you have allowed them in, we are not going to shoot them en masse; we are blocking that. But we were the first and only party that said: start hormone darts immediately.

You have seen that I stood up for our people. I will continue to do so. I have never called anyone, not even those you mention, a Nazi, fascist, or racist. That is of a completely different order. I will never do that either. If I had done that, you would never have accepted it either.

Thank you, Chair. As far as our group is concerned, this amendment did not need to be passed, because we are still eagerly awaiting the Spring Budget Memorandum. We do, of course, have a number of questions, but we believe that everything will work out well with the Spring Memorandum. Then everything will be resolved, because it will become clear what the effects of the cuts are, but also what will be invested in and where we are going to improve healthcare. That is good. I am eagerly looking forward to that, as I said. But now that I am standing here, I would still like to put a few questions to the Minister. The Minister is currently working on a guideline for craftsmanship and job satisfaction. In what way and within what timeframe is this guideline expected to contribute to limiting the labor market shortage? After all, it is in everyone's interest that labor market potential is not jeopardized by additional cuts. But how exactly is this guideline going to work, I ask the Minister. As far as the PVV group is concerned, there should also be a strong emphasis on tackling healthcare fraud. It is expected that approximately 10 billion euros in healthcare funds have been defrauded. In fact, that is actually just the tip of the iceberg. It is money that simply exists, but is in the wrong, or rather, the wrong places. The Minister will soon introduce the Act on the Integrity of Business Operations for Healthcare and Youth Care Providers to tackle fraud. But will this concretely ensure that fraudulent money ends up in the right place? Will enforcement, for example, be stricter? What successes have been achieved so far in detecting healthcare fraud, I ask the Minister. If there have been successes in recovering healthcare funds that have been defrauded, does that money return to the VWS budget — as I see no trace of it in the budget — or does it simply go to general funds? If that is the case, what will the Minister do to ensure that healthcare funds benefit healthcare and continue to do so? Finally, Chair. We can look forward to 600 million in investments in elderly care starting in 2027. Can the Minister already please our parliamentary group with the announcement that this money will be used for the rollout of small-scale residential centers for the elderly, in other words, for nursing homes 2.0? That’s all.

Chairperson. On behalf of the PVV as well, thanks to the Minister for the debate. The PVV advocates for a substantial increase in fines for employing illegal workers. Although an investigation into the level of the fines is currently underway, this investigation will not be completed until after the consideration of the SZW budget. Therefore, I advocate that the indexation of the fines take place as soon as possible, regardless of the outcome of the investigation. This relates to the amount of new fines and not to that of previously issued fines. The motion reads as follows: The House, having heard the deliberations, noting that the fines for companies that illegally employ workers from abroad are currently insufficient, as indicated by the Netherlands Labour Inspectorate; noting that in 90% of cases the fines can be recouped within a year, and that in 7 of the 24 situations examined this even occurs within three months or less; considering that the current fines are insufficiently deterrent and therefore do not constitute an effective means of combating illegal labour; whereas the maximum fine was set at €8,000 in 2005; requests the Government to ensure that the said fines are indexed retroactively as soon as possible, and to inform the House of the timetable for this, at the latest prior to the consideration of the SZW budget for 2025, and proceeds to the order of the day.

You are talking about years of PVV policy, but this minister hasn't even been minister for a year, so what you are saying is already incorrect. I have already answered. I can repeat that three more times, but ...

Mr. Boutkan asked who *will* be attending the 30th COP. It is true that I will not be going. Ultimately, it is up to the Ministers of Health, Welfare and Sport and of Foreign Affairs to provide you with further information on this matter. I do not know at this moment. As soon as a decision has been made, you will be informed.

Monitoring 17% does not mean that you are no longer going to do that 3%. Of course, we are going to see how it turns out. It is a huge operation. We are also going to work with tariff structures so that airlines are perhaps not forced, but at least enticed, to deploy their quietest aircraft at Schiphol. But these are, of course, estimates. We hope they will do so because those tariffs are set, but naturally, we do not know that for sure. So we have to see how this package plays out. That continues through monitoring the effectiveness of the measures. But that is, of course, completely separate from the question of whether I commit to 20%. Yes, I commit to 20%, namely to 17% in a large package and to 3% that we will fill in afterwards. But of course, we are going to see how the 17% turns out. Perhaps it is so effective that we have 18% instead of 17%. Then we only have 2% left to fill in. So, 20% noise reduction remains the goal of this cabinet. I agreed on that with all ministers. We are going to stick to it. But of course, we will monitor the situation and see how we are going to proceed. We are doing this in consultation with all parties.

Nice words, but it makes absolutely no sense. We have great respect for our voters. For more than 4 billion euros, we are going to lower the deductible by more than half. The PVV is very proud of that. That means that a great many people who are currently sick... You know: the GP is not included in the deductible, so people can go to the GP. But a great many studies show that if many people need expensive medication afterwards or have to see a specialist in the hospital, they currently do not do so because they simply do not have that €385. There are insurers who say you can pay that in twelve installments, but imagine having to pay an extra €100 together for a year for just a few months for that deductible, together with your partner. Elderly people and people with low incomes who are sick often stop going to the hospital. By lowering the deductible from €385 to €165 — again, that costs a lot of money: 4.3 billion — those people will be able to go to the hospital and receive care that they cannot afford now. Isn't that wonderful? Surely you can't attack me for that? That is something to be incredibly proud of. I think the people hearing this now are thinking: damn it, of course we would have preferred it to have happened tomorrow, but we are so glad the PVV pulled it off!

I also do not dispute that tackling the problem at the source is very important. I am therefore pleased that nearly 4 million vaccines are currently available from stock—so they are there—for the campaign in Africa. Ms. Paulusma reiterates here that the 13,200 she refers to have a limited shelf life. That is not limited, as they are valid until September 2025. That gives me the opportunity and the time to see how things develop over a few more months. In the event of an outbreak in the Netherlands, I can then make the choice to deploy those vaccines as well. That is therefore permitted. Once I have used up the Imvanex, I may use the other vaccines afterwards, because I can then request permission from the Inspectorate.

For the twenty-fifth time: the House is not being sidelined. How many times do I have to tell you and explain this? There is a Continuation Act. According to Article 110, paragraph 2 of the Aliens Act, this must be sent to the House immediately after the Royal Decree issued pursuant to Article 111 has been issued. The House can debate it immediately. If the House rejects it, everything lapses; naturally, I hope that does not happen. So you can continue spouting rehearsed sentences, but the House is NOT being sidelined. The House has the final word — as it should be, I say indeed as the Nestor of this House — and very quickly too, because the Continuation Act must be sent to the House immediately by the Prime Minister and the Cabinet. That must happen, so to speak, on the day the Royal Decree issued pursuant to Article 111 is issued.

The motion on document no. 99 by GroenLinks/Labour Party, submitted by Mr. De Hoop, requests the government to enter into discussions with ProRail, NS, and the Province of Zeeland to investigate whether the line can be connected to the HSL-Zuid and to inform the House regarding this. This motion is deemed "superfluous". The connection of the Zeeland line to the HSL is being investigated in step three of "Wind in de zeilen". This investigation will be launched in 2025. It will examine passenger numbers and the best routing. Naturally, we will continue to work with both the rail sector and the Zeeland parties to improve the reliability of the Zeeland railway, so the motion is superfluous. The motion on document no. 100 by Mr. Boutkan of the PVV asks that the nuisance caused by testing on the Zeeland line be kept to a minimum and that an analysis be carried out of the test on the Zeeland line on one of the two tracks, which must be shared with the House before the summer of 2025. I must advise against this motion. In the coming years, we will make every effort, together with the rail sector and the Zeeland parties, to limit the nuisance caused by the test track section as much as possible. Following the results of the study, it was also examined whether it would be possible to test on one of the two tracks, but this is not feasible. A comprehensive analysis will not change this assessment, but would require additional time from the experts. The motion regarding document no. 101 by Ms. Veltman, Mr. Grinwis, and Ms. Vedder receives the House's judgment. I also leave the motion regarding document no. 102 concerning an investigation into a high-speed bus between Vlissingen, Zierikzee, and Rotterdam to the House's judgment.

I don't want to answer that so matter-of-factly right away. What I mean by that—as I have already said—is that it is primarily up to the Minister of Foreign Affairs. He must issue an official report. I have to wait for that first, and based on that, we must see if there is room for Syrian asylum seekers to return to their country of origin. That is simply step one. That lies with the Minister of Foreign Affairs. Furthermore, diplomatic relations fall under the Minister of Foreign Affairs. It may sound a bit trivial, but in the first instance, you naturally ought to put that question to him. That is his portfolio. But the fact remains that we are going to look into possibilities—and not only the Netherlands is doing this; other European countries are doing it too—to allow Syrians to return to their country of origin.

Once again: it is total nonsense. I have never said anything other than that the criminals, the scum, must be denaturalized and expelled from the country. I never said that this applies to everyone. How did you come to that conclusion? Show me a single statement of mine in which I said that. You won't be able to find it. It is cheap debate rhetoric. I believe that those who commit criminal offenses must finally be dealt with to the maximum extent, must be arrested, must be convicted, and must receive a long prison sentence. And after that, they must lose their passports and, if at all possible, they must leave the country. But that only applies to people who have been convicted of criminal acts.

That seems clear to me from my answer. I have even given you more of an answer. Once again, it then goes to the IND and the IND assesses it. There is a very high chance that they will be allowed to stay. Yes, so that seems like an answer to me. At that moment, that person is a refugee, but when the country of origin is safe, we will work on ensuring that this refugee returns.

In my opinion, Mr. Dijk is also responding to the situation with Co-Med, but Co-Med is not private equity. Co-Med is owned by a private investor. So that is not private equity. Naturally, I do not want that situation with Co-Med to return. Patients are the victims of that, so we do not want that. What we are going to do in the Healthcare Institutions Ethical Conduct Act is to curb the locusts and the people who are making a mess of things and who are simply out for profit.

I must also advise against the motion by Member Thijssen regarding document no. 193. The concerns are known to my colleague at the Ministry of Social Affairs and Employment. I must also refer to the Ministry of Social Affairs and Employment regarding that plan of action. It is, of course, important that all companies comply with laws and regulations. This applies to every company, and PostNL is no exception.

We believe we can achieve the same result by building in flexibility. Therefore, I must advise against the motion. The motion regarding document no. 286 by Mr. Heutink of the PVV requests the government to make every effort to reinstate as many cancelled sailings as possible into Doeksen's timetable. I pass judgment on that motion, provided I may interpret it to mean that we are entering into dialogue with the shipping company to realize as many sailings as possible in the timetable within the available possibilities. I understand that this is a partial answer.

Thank you, Chair. Thanks also to the committee members. I will start with the questions from the VVD, from Ms. Veltman. She is actually asking me three questions. They were packaged into a single question, but anyway. There were three parts. My answer is as follows. In mid-June — June 12th, I recall — I will have a meeting with the municipalities that have proceeded with the introduction of zero-emission zones, so then I can provide a more detailed answer to the questions being asked now. All vehicles in a zero-emission zone must simply comply with the rules and can be fined by enforcement officers. I will also go into this in more detail shortly during the assessment of the motion from the PVV.

You have been to the islands yourself. I have also been to the islands and spoken with people there. They consider other matters more important. They would rather have bread on the table than a monument on the corner, so to speak, because a third of the population lives below the poverty line. Do you think they care if a podcast is recorded somewhere where people can air their grievances about the history of slavery? That doesn't interest them at all.

That was two years ago. I saw that video message too. But times change, don't they? I think there is a very different sentiment in society now than in 2022. So I actually think it is perfectly fine. I suspect no demonstrations against the Golden Coach will be organized. If they are, they should keep their distance. Hasn't a new era simply dawned, in which we can say: we are all moving past the unpleasant discussion that was attached to the coach, as if it were a promotion of slavery or something similar? That has all been thoroughly investigated and researched. It has been stated that the coach is precisely not a promotion of slavery. You cannot erase the past. I said that in my speech as well. Isn't it a perfect moment to bring out the beautiful Golden Coach again on Prinsjesdag—which practically all of the Netherlands loved and still loves? It wasn't exhibited for nothing, and it is currently in the stables. I think that is a shame. It is a beautiful coach with a fantastic history and tradition. We have a new cabinet and these are new times; it is time for the Golden Coach again. I would seize the moment.

I conclude the following. If the Distribution Act is repealed before the end of the year, in accordance with the wishes of these coalition parties, you will not become the Minister of Agriculture, Nature and Environment, but the Minister for lining the pockets of expensive hotels and cruise ships. For that is what you will have to do, or you will have to disregard the law. Nevertheless, I would like to ask the coalition parties to stand up for this. This also applies to my colleague from the PVV, who said yesterday: we do not want to line the pockets of expensive hotels and cruise ships at all. For if the Distribution Act is repealed, the consequence is that accommodation must be arranged and therefore purchased in accordance with the law. Then you will end up with expensive emergency shelter, and the policy will therefore be more expensive, probably even more expensive than that of your predecessor. That would then be PVV policy, perpetuated by these coalition parties.

We must never consider lying to elected representatives in the House and failing to answer questions normal; we must never accept that, I also say to the spokespersons for the NSC and the VVD. Chair. For seven months, this Minister has done little to combat nuisance and ensure peace in Ter Apel, to which those people are entitled. Now there are many words, but actions failed to materialize all those months. This Minister is just like the PVV: many words, few deeds. Chair. It is a political choice to leave the people in Ter Apel, staff and fellow residents, in misery for all those months. It is a political choice that I find unacceptable. I have co-signed various motions, because the House was very united today. That is precisely why it is so bitter that this debate turned into such a disaster again. I have co-signed: the motion by Ms. Rajkowski, the motion by Mr. Boomsma, the motion by Ms. Piri, and the motion by Ms. Podt. All good proposals. I will add another proposal of my own. The House, having heard the deliberations, considering that the Ter Apel registration centre has been overcrowded and overburdened for years and that the village of Ter Apel has borne the burden as the largest registration centre in the Netherlands for long enough; requests the Government to carefully phase out the registration centre in Ter Apel to a scale that better suits the surroundings and public support, for example by opening two smaller registration centres in other locations, preferably in wealthy municipalities that do not yet contribute to the reception facilities, and proceeds to the order of the day.

Yes, once again, the choice we have now made, and which I support, is to do both, but in a different way than you suggest. Initially, with a very ambitious program—again, the Netherlands has never seen anything like it; it is historic—we are setting up the strictest asylum policy ever. And for criminals, we are going to look at whether we can amend the treaty and the law in such a way that we send them back. That may not be exactly what you want, but I think it is the wisest route.

I do not agree with that. If I were to make such a decision, I would want it to be financially covered. Otherwise, the decision is pointless. Naturally, we must make a decision that is technically feasible and affordable. Everything needs to be arranged; otherwise, a decision is meaningless. Making a decision is not the goal. The goal is the preservation of the lighthouse. We have already stated that renovating and stabilizing seems best to us. It could have been otherwise. It could also have been in a state where you say: we see no future in it anymore; demolition is the only solution. We did not say that. So, that is good news. But how such a cast-iron tower can be preserved is still very complicated. It simply takes some time. I am going to send you a letter outlining what we are currently working on. Ultimately, a decision will have to be made, but it must be a financially covered decision.

Next, the motion by Mr. Ram. That is the motion regarding document no. 3095. It requests the government to advocate for a further simplification of Omnibus and CSDDD. The Omnibus proposal focuses precisely on the goal of reducing administrative burdens for businesses. That is also a priority of the Cabinet. Consequently, the Cabinet has broadly welcomed the Omnibus proposal. That position is also stated in the BNC fiche. I must advise against further simplification; that is not the Cabinet's position. The motion regarding document no. 3095 is therefore advised against. Next, the motion regarding document no. 3096 by De Korte and Ceder. It requests that the impact be mapped out. I have already promised this during the committee debate, so I can give the House a judgment on the motion. Then, the next motion. That is the motion regarding document no. 3097 by De Korte concerning parcels. It asks to advocate in the Council for Foreign Affairs and Trade for the reintroduction of bulk controls and for strengthening the enforcement capacity of national customs authorities. Ms. De Korte raises an important issue, and I can therefore give the House my judgment on this motion.

Chair. Gambling constitutes a legal market. In the State Secretary's view, the economic reality appears to be underemphasized, whereas a healthy, competitive legal sector is precisely necessary to discourage illegal supply. The PVV urges a more balanced approach from the State Secretary. This also applies to raising the age from 18 to 21 for certain games of chance. As far as the PVV is concerned, this is not only patronizing but will also be counterproductive. Therefore, the following motion. The House, having heard the debate, noting that the State Secretary intends to raise the minimum age for participation in certain games of chance from 18 to 21; whereas in the Netherlands, 18 is broadly used as the age at which people reach the age of majority and acquire full legal capacity; whereas raising the minimum age could lead to an increase in illegal gambling, thereby denying players access to addiction prevention and consumer protection; whereas stricter enforcement and better education are more effective instruments to combat gambling addiction among young adults than raising the age; requests the Government to refrain from raising the minimum age for gambling to 21 and instead focus on strengthened enforcement and prevention, and proceeds to the order of the day.

Thank you, Chair. Late last year, The Hague and the Netherlands were shocked by the horrific attack in Mariahoeve, in which several people lost their lives. It now appears that a week earlier, the police arrested the suspect of the Tarwekamp attack with a car full of jerrycans and heavy fireworks, and released him shortly thereafter. This obviously cannot be true. The PVV therefore wants a debate with the Minister of Justice to find out how on earth this could have happened.
But did that David actually survive that, killing a lion and a bear with a stone? Because that would take a few years, I think.
Whether or not your home in your home country is in good condition is, of course, not a condition for asylum. I have not yet seen that in the IND's policy. It is not standing policy.

Thank you, Chair. I will begin by assessing the motions. I must advise against the motion on document no. 625 by Member Thijssen regarding advocating for greater protection of Dutch and European interests. This is because the open economy is naturally important for the Netherlands, as is the internal market. "Made in Europe" or "made in the Netherlands" can be contrary to the principles of the WTO. Naturally, I am already committed to protecting the interests of the Cabinet's vision on EU competitiveness. I can, however, support the spirit of Member Thijssen's motion. After all, we are already working within a European context to ensure a stronger Europe, for example through procurement rules.

Thank you, Chair. The values of human dignity, equality, and solidarity are the yardstick by which my party assesses developments and formulates alternatives. This Trade and Development Aid budget runs directly contrary to these values. Cutting billions in the coming years is incredibly shortsighted. The biggest cut to development cooperation ever, we have already heard the PVV proudly proclaim. Women's rights, girls' rights, humanitarian aid, human rights—the axe is being wielded. I therefore call upon the Minister to reconsider and not do this. We must discuss the consequences of this cabinet's dismantling here today. Research by the Guttmacher Institute from last month shows some of the consequences of the cuts to Dutch aid for women's rights and sexual health. For every 10 million euros in cuts, approximately 900,000 women will no longer have access to contraception. It could lead to 273,000 more unwanted pregnancies, 17,000 fewer people receiving medication for HIV, and 466,000 people who are not tested for or receive information about HIV. What is the Minister's view on these consequences? We have a proposal to take these cuts off the table, and the same applies to the cuts to humanitarian aid. Earlier this week, it was added to this that the Minister plans to deliberately impose impossible demands on civil society organizations. These are organizations that, for example, provide support and protection to women's and human rights defenders. The House recently adopted a motion to support the work of women's rights and female human rights activists, but the Minister's goal seems to be to let these organizations collapse. Is that indeed this Minister's objective? I would like an answer. For, if so, why is the Minister suddenly acting directly against the wishes of this House? This concerns the adopted proposal I just mentioned, but also a proposal we adopted here just last month, namely to invest specifically in civil society. If letting organizations collapse is not this minister's goal, what consequences does the minister think her policy will have? I am very curious about that. Something else this cabinet claims to consider important: the coalition program states that it wants to promote stability in vulnerable countries. Conflicts are being fought and fueled with weapons all over the world. If you value stability and peace, control over our arms exports should not be relinquished. Just today, it became clear that if we give control away to France, for example, our weapons and weapon parts simply end up in a country like Sudan, where a bloody war is being fought with them. We have also already seen that the court overruled this cabinet regarding the delivery of F-35 parts to Israel. Those deliveries should not have taken place, and still should not. Is the minister now going to regularly review the years of general licenses on arms exports? Is this cabinet going to take a step back regarding accession to the Aachen Treaty? In October, the House adopted a motion by the SP to advocate for a UN arms embargo for the entire country of Sudan. Our motion to halt arms trade to Sudan via third countries was also adopted. The Minister indicates that she has followed up on this by mentioning it once in international forums. I assume, however, that the Minister's efforts will go further than that. What else is she going to do to stop the arms trade to Sudan? Is she prepared to ensure that additional humanitarian aid reaches Sudan, because it is desperately needed for tens of millions of people who would otherwise starve to death? If not, we have a proposal for that as well. The role of women in peace processes has also been discussed here previously. It has been demonstrated that this is so incredibly important and so incredibly effective. Is the Minister prepared to preserve the Women, Peace and Security fund and not cut it short? Thank you, Chair.

I understand that, but that is something else entirely. The 45-minute standard is yet another funding contribution. In a situation where we are going to adjust the hospital landscape—which we are going to do—with distribution and budget funding for intensive care units, emergency departments, and acute obstetrics, more hospitals will qualify than the 30 to 31 that currently fall under the 45-minute standard. In essence, the 45-minute standard is being absorbed into this. I am currently drafting a letter regarding this, which I will send to the House in the coming days. Mr. Claassen asked about the effects of the amendment to the Decree on Acute Care. All hospitals with an Emergency Department, Intensive Care Unit, or acute obstetrics department will soon receive budget funding. At present, only hospitals sensitive to the 45-minute standard receive an availability contribution for the Emergency Department and acute obstetrics. By adjusting the 45-minute standard, all hospitals will become sensitive. I just explained that. This means that all hospitals considering limiting the provision of acute care must make every effort to keep these departments open. The Decree on Acute Care (AMvB) and its elaboration in the ministerial regulation specify the decision-making process hospitals must follow if they are considering adjusting the supply. It states with whom the hospital must enter into dialogue, and the hospital must indicate what it intends to do with the input from the stakeholders. First, I will prepare a guideline to further promote the dialogue in the region. After that, we will proceed with the regulations.

The motion regarding document no. 942 is from Ms. Van Zanten. She requested the government to evaluate the effectiveness of the border controls carried out and to present an improvement plan before the internal border controls are extended. The Cabinet has already decided to extend the internal border controls until 9 December 2025. Your House was informed of this decision and the substantiation on 25 April. In the coming period, the Royal Netherlands Marechaussee will continue to carry out internal border controls within its existing capacity. I have also previously promised to inform the House about this, so I advise against this motion. Then I have …

The Minister is now saying: to deport foreign nationals, we can simply settle it with a summary penal order. However, the Circular on Aliens states that for this, a serious crime must have been committed for which the individual has been sentenced to a prison term or a custodial measure. The very problem is that such a summary penal order does not allow for a custodial measure or a prison term. If the Minister maintains this, he is sidelining the Dutch Cabinet or the Dutch Minister for Asylum and Migration regarding the deportation of asylum seekers who have committed criminal acts and have been convicted for them. That is simply stated in the Circular on Aliens; I am not making this up. The Minister must therefore stick to the facts.

Yes, I advise against the amendment. The intention of the proposed legislative changes is to provide a legal basis for the current practice as it is currently carried out by the Inspectorate, Veilig Thuis, and so on. If we do not do that, the possibility of working in this way will cease to exist. I consider that unwise. The reason why professional secrecy and the protectability of medical data are being overruled is solely to protect patients, to protect children, and to protect vulnerable individuals. These are people who may be victims of abuse or violence. Those are good, weighty reasons to overrule that. That is why I have adopted this legislation. It is to ensure that we properly establish the legal foundations for current practice.

No, we have brought along excellent ministers who are doing their very best. They are ensuring that hospital care is brought back into order, as far as possible. They are ensuring that the influx of asylum seekers decreases; that has also happened. During the negotiations on the Spring Budget, they are — and this is also my responsibility in the coming weeks — working hard on energy bills, trying to do something about grocery prices, and are going to do their best to propose not letting rents rise so much. We are doing all of that. But we do have to do it with four people. It is not all free, after all. Politics is also a matter of choice. You just said to a colleague: everything is political; one person might have *this* reason and another might have *that* reason. That applies here too. You know, if you have €100, you have to choose what to spend it on. The four parties think differently about that. That is the case, Mr. Dijk, when you are in a coalition. You haven't experienced that yet, but that is the way it is when you are in a coalition. So it is easy to just say, "Let's do that tomorrow." I would like to do that. I did that too when I was sitting in your chair. But now we have made agreements. We have to honor them. I have to fight every single day to make that happen, and I am going to do so. I am going to fight hard. If it is not enough, then we have a problem.

Thank you, Mr. Messenger. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I think that if we continue like this, our elderly and the less resilient will also end up behind fences. I think that when I myself am old and no longer resilient, I would also like to live protected behind fences. In some parts of the world, unfortunately, that is already the case, with all the riffraff walking around there. Mr. Chairman. I find it very risky to play God. I have said that before. I am a religious person. I am also truly shocked by the SGP every time. That used to be somewhat my party — I think the ChristenUnie handles this better — but these are all beings created by Our Lord, even in the faith of the SGP. Lately, I myself have also been increasingly inclined to believe in the theory of evolution, but what is happening is very risky. And believe me, you will get it back someday. If you treat animals in an undignified manner, you will get that back someday. According to my faith, there is a divine court. I also wish it upon everyone who is bad to animals to be caught by the divine court. Ten years ago, the PVV was the only party not happy with the return ...

I believe it is my turn to speak. I have just outlined the approach we have and intend to implement. I have to start somewhere. Look, ideally, I would just like this person to be taken off the street immediately. I would prefer to put him on the first plane back, but I have to adhere to regulations. You might say that it is laxer, but it is not laxer at all. I have certainly tightened things up as well. I would like to explain that to you in more detail. Concrete measures have also been taken in Ter Apel. I found out about something based on a tip from someone. Then I started investigating. What was going on in Ter Apel? There were people reporting during the night or after 10:00 PM. At that time, the registration and identification lane was closed. We were then obliged to admit them. They were not registered; they just walked in. This means that there were people walking around whose identities we had no idea about. The impression was also created that people were using this facility to come and spend the night. That was the standard practice there. I found out about it and called the COA immediately, and within half an hour on the phone, I had a plan: "We are going to do this differently." In other words, a pilot is currently running that, as far as I am concerned, should be continued. That pilot entails identification and registration taking place 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, so that we know who is there. It also turned out there was quite a hassle with keys and passes: it appeared that people were sleeping in beds on the premises who didn't belong there at all. They were sitting there with someone else's key and pass. I called and asked the COA if that was correct. "Yes, that is correct." I said: "Is that correct? Why isn't anything being done about it?" "Yes, yes…" I said: "What do you do then?" "We change the keys every now and then." I said: "Yeah, hello! That's not how it works. That's not how we operate. I want a pass system so the door opens just like in a hotel." So, that is what we have started doing now. As soon as possible, we are going to link those passes to biometric features. I just call them "the illegal sleepers"; yes, I have to give them a name. They don't belong there at all, but they are sleeping there. So that is what I am tackling.

It is not just a plan that goes into effect this coming Monday; it is actually going to happen. The Royal Netherlands Marechaussee (KMar) is well-trained to stop the right vehicles and the right people. In doing so, we are also trying to relieve the regions as much as possible, so that normal commuter traffic at this border experiences minimal disruption. However, the moment suspicious activity arises, vehicles and individuals will certainly be stopped. We are not always present at every border post, but we act selectively and based on intelligence. It depends on the situation. Based on that, we will staff certain posts more frequently and conduct additional checks.

Yes, anything could have happened. I could have been wearing a hat, too. I could have done all sorts of things here. I did what I just did. I think that is a very good way to approach this process. We received the letter yesterday. You know, I see points of contact in it. So I try to be constructive. I believe we have the same goal, but I am approaching it our way and you are standing there shouting now. That is fine.

That is the PVV we know, I think. They pull all sorts of tricks out of their bag of tricks and resort to diversionary tactics. Today, we are discussing the budget for the Ministry of the Interior here. We are talking about people. We are talking about municipalities being pushed into the abyss. We are talking about 150,000 children living in poverty. The PVV does not want to reduce this, yet says very proudly: we are stabilizing it, we are leaving things just as they are. The PVV has no solutions. And every time they have no solutions for the problems of the people in the Netherlands, they pull a trick out of their bag of tricks, a diversionary tactic. Because you know very well that you do not decide whether to dismiss a minister or appoint one. I want you to return to the substance: what are you going to do, concretely do, to support municipalities? What are you going to do to help 150,000 children living in poverty? I would like to hear from you about that, and I am questioning you on that.

Against Zuyderland and in favor of private equity—that is how Mr. Claassen of the PVV aptly summarized the contribution of their own PVV Minister of Health today, I would say. It indeed appears that private equity has little to fear from this minister, and that the House will once again have to correct this minister to prevent our healthcare from becoming further commercialized. That is why I have three proposals here. The House, having heard the deliberations, noting that directors of healthcare entrepreneurs such as Co-Med and care agency Victorie enriched themselves with public funds through expense fraud, mismanagement, deception, and the spending of enormous profits partly derived from subsidies; noting that 10 billion euros of public money flows away annually to dubious profit distributions, dividend payments, and fraud in healthcare, yet prosecution cannot always be initiated, for example, because the source of the profits cannot be demonstrated; calls upon the government to ensure that every euro can be recovered in cases of fraud and distributed profits if it has been established that healthcare providers did not provide good quality of care, violated billing rules, or withdrew funds from the organization while the financial situation was demonstrably poor, resulting in bankruptcy, and proceeds to the order of the day.

This is my final interruption, then. You may say that; but then you must do it. Yes, the SP believes that those status holders also have a right to housing. We believe that everyone has a right to a home. We have said and agreed on that here as well: an end to homelessness by 2030. But the very concrete question to the PVV is not whether you want to show up here every time to say "how unfair that Dutch people don't get priority status." The question to the PVV is: what are you going to do about it? We already have the proposals ready. Is the PVV going to support them, yes or no? Or are we going to hear the same story here every time: "oh, how unfair that this group doesn't get priority status"?

If you start a war, you shouldn't complain when you lose it. On October 7, a year and a half ago, we saw Hamas commit the most brutal, disgusting, sick murder of innocent Jewish people from Gaza. Since then, during that horrific war, we have seen that the Palestinians, and Hamas in particular, have barely kept their agreements. Just a few weeks ago, we even saw Hamas detonate bombs on buses in five Israeli cities during the so-called ceasefire. We saw that they unilaterally initiated a sort of Ramadan ceasefire, as they called it themselves, to try to build up a terrorist force this month, the month of March. We saw that they did not keep a single agreement to resume exchanges or to restore normal life to the people they held hostage. They have failed to honor the agreements regarding the men, women, children, and the elderly held hostage by the terrorist scum of Hamas. They have not honored those agreements. They have detonated bombs in Israeli cities. So, of course, I regret every innocent death, and I regret it when innocent people lose their lives. But I do say that I understand very well why Israel is continuing this war, that it does not accept that the Hamas scum, who have held their people hostage, who have caused people to die, and who have ensured that there are still — how many are there? — 40 or 50 people in captivity under the leadership of Hamas… I understand very well that it does not accept that this is being bargained over. And I tell you that this war will not be over until Hamas is completely eradicated. That is what must happen. They are terrorists. It is impossible to do business with them. Action must be taken against them. Of course, this is preferably done with as few civilian casualties as possible, but I support the action against Hamas one hundred percent.

I lead a cabinet of four parties, and I do not lead a PVV cabinet. That is a misrepresentation of the facts. There are four parties in this cabinet. I am the non-partisan Prime Minister of this cabinet, and I implement policies that are supported by those four parties, and therefore not by just one.

We are working with a great cabinet, with great measures. Not all of them are painless. You are right about that. For example, that includes 600 million extra going to elderly care. You conveniently choose not to mention that. But there is pain involved too. Cuts are indeed being made to curative care, with all sorts of framework plans or whatever they are called. So it is not all a party. I agree with you on that. But we are getting a lot done. I told you that last time as well. We managed to get the deductible frozen by 60%. That does indeed lead to an increase in the premium, but that is compensated for by lower income tax — on which we are spending about 3.5 billion — by lower premiums for entrepreneurs, and partly by a higher healthcare allowance. So for the first time in history, that deductible is going down by 60% thanks to the PVV, after we freeze it for the next two years. Well, the SP never managed to achieve that. We achieved that for the people. Moreover, we have allocated another two times 2.5 billion for tax relief for those same people in 2025 and 2026, because the reduction of the deductible will not be implemented for another two years. That amounts to a total of 5 billion in tax relief for citizens. We are increasing the housing allowance. We are increasing the child budget. A quarter of a billion and a half a billion will be allocated structurally for the groups in distress. We are lowering the energy tax for people at home. The fuel excise duty reduction will simply continue for another year starting January 1st. So it will not be increased. We also have another 2 billion in tax relief for purchasing power for the working population — also a very important group in the Netherlands. Childcare remains free. There will be compensation for students. Mr. Chairman. I will stop now, because I see that Mr. Dijk already regrets his question. But there are many moments and many measures that we are taking specifically for the people. And you are sitting there in the opposition, doing absolutely nothing for the people.

As you know, the ACM has independent authority, and I have no insight into how it intends to allocate capacity. I would be happy to ask whether they have provided for a capacity split between big tech and small, whether established in the Netherlands or not. I can, of course, ask that question if it provides you with comfort during the process. I do not decide on the answer, but I would naturally like to pass the question on.

The PVV also considers this very important. However, we also have a committee debate on the Council for Competitiveness coming up soon. That concerns the internal market. The document for which you are requesting this debate really needs to be submitted to Brussels, so there is no support at this moment. I would very much like to include it in the committee debate on the Council for Competitiveness. I would, however, like to ask the Minister for a response to this document.

I come from there, of course. It is, naturally, a former colleague of mine who wrote that letter. I know the situation in Rotterdam well, of course. I also believe that it is feasible in those major cities, but the point is precisely that we are compiling a list here for all regions, not just for the major cities, but also for South Limburg and Northwest Groningen. That is exactly what this is about. We believe that it is sufficiently clear. We do not fully acknowledge the concerns that exist in those three cities, but regarding that too, I have said that I think it is sensible to proceed in this manner, including these forms of care that have already been included. But we are, of course, keeping a finger on the pulse. I have also told the VNG that. We will, of course, closely monitor what happens. Should there be grounds to make changes, we will do so. I have also promised the House that we will submit any potential amendment to the Order in Council for approval again.

Yes. Then… I hear Mr. Bushoff off-microphone asking if he is still getting an answer. Yes, but then I am going to repeat that funds are available via C-support in any case, and that funds are available via the post-COVID clinics, for which you yourselves made the money available. It is a new disease. We must move as quickly as possible towards a normalized situation where post-COVID care is funded under the regular Health Insurance Act.

Chair, thank you. This debate takes us back to one of the darkest periods in recent history: the rise and fall of the Islamic State in Iraq and Syria. It was a time of the most heinous crimes against humanity, beheadings, slavery, and burning alive. It was a time of the caliphate, an ultimate evil with which no compromise was possible and which had to be fought until it was eradicated root and branch. Chair. To that end, the soldiers of our Kingdom were called to arms in 2014. In the years that followed, they fought like true lions in the Levant. 3,000 missions were carried out against IS, in which the weapons of our armed forces were deployed over 2,100 times to defeat the enemy. This, together with the efforts of our allies, led to the territorial destruction of the caliphate in 2019. The population was liberated from brutal oppression. But, Chair, the bravery of our soldiers stood in stark contrast to the cowardice of the administrators at the time. The report we are discussing here today is a scathing judgment on their conduct. Time and again, they lied to the House—and thereby to the Dutch people—about the terrible incident in Hawija. It is good that this investigation has now been conducted and that this Cabinet is learning from it. But, Chair, anyone who hoped to finally be able to close this file will be disappointed. For new facts are surfacing once again, under a VVD Minister of Defence. The investigators repeatedly asked for the Dutch video footage from after the attack, but were told that it no longer existed. But when de Volkskrant recently wrote an article about it, the minister suddenly sprang into action and the footage was found in no time. This is an unacceptable state of affairs, about which the PVV wants clarification. Chair. This minister has indicated that, as far as he is concerned, the millions of euros in compensation already given to the Iraqis are not yet sufficient, and that he is investigating whether he can dump even more money across the border. May I remind this minister that this cabinet is actually making drastic cuts to development aid? If he wishes to play the Good Samaritan himself, he should do so from his own generous salary, but not from the money intended for the reconstruction of our own armed forces. For, Mr. Chairman, there is only one group that should be able to count on extra care and appreciation, and that is not the Iraqis, whom we liberated from IS, mind you, but our own veterans, who fought this gigantic battle. Their sacrifices remain largely unmentioned, and they deserve much more recognition than is currently the case. The PVV therefore calls upon this minister to dedicate himself to these heroes, for it was they who, at the risk of their own lives, defended civilization against darkness. We must never, ever forget that. Thank you very much.

Yes. Then I will start with a question from member Bamenga. If there are no legal concerns, may we then assume… Yes, I have actually already discussed that. We are going to continue this Balanced Approach with 15% and then another 5%. I do not want to do that in any other way. I see no possibilities for that.
