How are we ultimately going to reach that 20%? I have already said that as well. This question is from member Grinwis. Then I have a question regarding an impact analysis of the choices and the impending excise duty increase. We are going to commission a study into the effects on prices. I hope that this is sufficient, in this case for Mr. Grinwis. Then another question from Mr. Grinwis: do we want, in the interest of Defence, as a precondition above… Yes, that concerns Lelystad Airport, which Defence has also set its sights on. Just as with civil aviation, I do not want to preempt decision-making. There are wishes, also from Lelystad. In 2025, the cabinet will make a decision regarding that airport, whether it is for military, civil aviation, or both.

Of course, it is going to work the other way around. There will soon be a financial framework that determines what we are going to do. That is where my concern lies. Suppose you just say: "We will ensure that the money is available that we need to strengthen the asylum chain. We will ensure that the money is available for the COA and the IND, so that the IND can process that enormous queue of people and also send people back if necessary." If you can guarantee me that, I will be much more reassured. That skeleton in the closet will still be there, but at least for the next two years we will know that there is enough money to get the chain in order. That is the crucial question. I understand everything else, but that does not change my observation. I want the guarantee that we are not deliberately making a huge — I am searching for a word; I wanted to say it in Rotterdam South terms — mess of it. Because that is what is going to happen. That is what I am afraid of.

The only thing I can promise is "as soon as possible." I cannot attach a timeframe to that, because that would be talking nonsense. Of course, I am also dependent on what comes in and how that develops. Naturally, I am doing everything I can to reduce the influx. That is necessary. That influx must go down. The whole situation is becoming unsustainable. But I cannot set a timeframe for that.

It is simply not true that this cabinet is doing nothing about it. We are taking all kinds of measures. I already said that child poverty and poverty in general are an extraordinarily complex problem. Previous cabinets have regularly struggled with it. In any case, this cabinet has the objective that it must not rise compared to the year 2024. Again, every person living in poverty is actually one too many. In that sense, I understand you. I also understand that you say that more should actually be done. But we are dealing with a whole package. In any case, we have ensured—we have adjusted it for that purpose as well—that poverty, specifically child poverty, does not rise. It was already an unimaginable task to achieve that. That is the situation on paper, by the way; those are calculations. Am I proud of that? On the one hand, yes; on the other hand, no. After all, you know that there are still so many people living in poverty. But that really cannot be solved by saying now that the cabinet needs to do more about it. Then poverty has not suddenly disappeared.
That is 2.3 billion from co-payments. So, on balance, it is 4.3 billion euros net for lowering the deductible and 600 million for housing for the elderly.

Fortunately, I do not calculate the funding myself. We have our accountants at the NZa for that. So, the NZa calculates the funding. Following previous interruptions by Ms. Tielen, I have already stated that we are also dealing with the prevailing situation. So much has been centralized in recent years that our regional hospitals have become increasingly unstable. Although a great many people still work there with great pleasure, this closed, that closed, and yet another entire hospital closed down. I am going to turn that around. For the first time, we are going to remove parts of hospital care from market forces and spread the budgets. The NZa, as the implementing body, is going to do that for us.

You know, in politics, it is sometimes also a matter of having faith in things that still need to be worked out. If you don't have confidence in the country's government, there is no end to it. Of course, I understand the SP. It is good to be critical of that. I share some of your concerns as well. I believe I just said that too. But the gain for us is, at the very least, that something from the election manifesto—those care homes 2.0—let me just call it that—will actually be realized. That is what we want to aim for regarding elderly care. And yes, money is being cut in a lot of places; that is the fact. In some areas, that is a thorn in our side as well. On the other hand, we have also said: it has to stop somewhere. I believe our parliamentary group leader conveyed that today as well.

You know as well as I do that I cannot say that, because before you know it, something like that happens again and then I will be standing here having to admit that I was wrong. But I am going to do whatever it takes to achieve that. As far as Co-Med is concerned, we saw a somewhat longer period during which the first signs emerged that things were not going well. We saw the NZa and the Inspectorate take action. At a certain point, we saw the situation arise where the health insurer terminated the contract. Then the bankruptcy followed a few weeks later. The health insurer ensured in advance that these 38,000 patients could access a digital GP as an emergency measure. That is, of course, not perfect. Naturally, you would have wanted these 38,000 patients to have gone from one regular GP to another. I would have liked to see that, of course. But the health insurer did not have that option at that time. As I also indicated just now, it is true that people now have a regular GP again, with the exception of a few. However, the health insurer could not arrange that at the time. I also understand that it is very difficult for an elderly couple to suddenly be confronted with a digital GP. That seems particularly unpleasant to me, perhaps certainly if you are not very digitally skilled. It is true, however, that if people needed to be seen, this was done via this digital route, and they could go to a GP in the neighborhood. The situation is not ideal. I readily admit that. But the health insurer considered the situation to have become untenable and terminated the contract for that reason.

We attach great importance to preventing impunity for the horrific crimes committed by IS. We remain committed to the prosecution and trial of IS fighters. We are still exploring options and will, of course, keep the House informed, as also promised by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs during the debate on the UN General Assembly.

Then I also assume that this cabinet, as soon as it gets to work on this — the words are on paper now, only there is no budget attached — will at some point say: we can do this and that, but we need tens of billions for it. Then the PVV will naturally go along with that.

I will send the House a letter stating when that lock will actually open completely, or failing that, with a timeframe for which vessels, as it may well be phased. Then there is the question from Member Heutink regarding the water taxis at night. I actually just stated that safety is a top priority in that regard. I also understand the desire for the water taxi. Then a question from Member De Hoop regarding the approach to the exploration concerning the accessibility of Ameland. That approach is currently being further developed. I would like to include Member De Hoop's suggestions in that development. On November 14 last, the House was informed about the start decision for the MIRT exploration into Ameland Accessibility. This also includes the measures that will be investigated. I will therefore also include Member De Hoop's suggestions. Finally, I have a question from Member Stoffer regarding Kornwerderzand. Am I making sufficient efforts to find a solution? I understand the ambition for Kornwerderzand very well. It is a broad ambition for a wider navigation channel; A wider lock, I must say. However, I do not yet have the necessary funds. We can have many wishes, but they will also have to be paid for. I have not found a solution for that yet, I tell Mr. Stoffer honestly. So I understand the ambition and I share it, but I do not have the financial solution yet, so we will continue working on this.

Chair. On the same day that Kristallnacht was commemorated, we saw Muslims hunting Jews in the streets of Amsterdam. A pogrom of the worst kind, and this time not by Nazis, but by criminal scum. RTL News quoted the following from one of the chat groups: "We are the new Moroccan generation and we will destroy you," referring to the Jews from Israel. Moroccans who want to destroy Jews: it is antisemitism in its worst form. They were dressed in black, with hoods up and Palestinian flags on their backs, in groups, on foot or on scooters, in cars, assisted by taxi drivers who indicated the locations where the Jews were located. It was premeditated and it was prepared. It makes me angry and sick to my stomach. What kind of goddamn country has this become, Chair, where Jews are once again being hunted in the streets, where Jews once again have to go into hiding to be safe? What kind of country has this become, where Jewish men, women, girls, the elderly, and children are forced to run for their lives, crying in fear, simply because they are Jewish, are beaten up, assaulted, stabbed with knives, thrown into the water, and even—we have all seen the videos—kicked in the head while already lying on the ground, while the perpetrators shout Arab and Palestinian slogans? People were forced to shout "Free Palestine," had to show their ID to prove they are not Jewish. It is a miracle that no one was killed during this raid, this jihad in the streets of beautiful old Amsterdam, which last week looked more like ISIS territory. And how in heaven's name is it possible that during that evening and night after that football match, none of those Islamic Jew hunters were arrested? Not a single arrest during the night itself! They are still walking around free, unpunished. Our rule of law is already torn apart and lying in the trash can, and will remain there as long as the perpetrators of these acts of terror remain unpunished. And it just keeps going. The day before yesterday, they set a tram on fire while shouting "cancerous Jews." It is anarchy in Amsterdam. The scum are in charge.

No. In my policy, as stated in the coalition programme, we are going to make multi-year financial agreements between health insurers and hospitals. At the same time, Zuyderland has already made the decision. So I am going to make one final attempt to change their minds, but they have already made the decision.

Perhaps a remark. I could of course postpone the motion, but I still wanted to say something. Anything is possible. We clone sheep, we land on the moon: anything is possible. And the Netherlands can do anything, so we don't always have to wait for Brussels. Thank you.

No, this is simply a matter of market forces. I cannot look into the stocks of other countries. I hope I have sufficiently answered Mr. Krul's question. That concerned the donation and how many vaccines are needed. Then there is the request for assistance regarding logistics. I have answered that as well. My colleague for BHO is making a donation for transport and training people. MP Jansen asked how likely it is that the Jynneos vaccine will be used. I cannot make that estimate. That is shrouded in uncertainty. I do not know when the new vaccines will be delivered. The estimate is in six months. I think it is a reasonably stable estimate that it will be spring 2025, simply because we have a contract. But the vaccines are currently sold out and I have no delivery date. That is, once again, the uncertainty inherent in many points. The Netherlands is prepared. The Municipal Health Services (GGDs) are prepared. El Abassi asked if I realize that viruses do not have a passport. Yes, I do realize that. I have also indicated several times why I endorse the general basic principle of infectious disease control that it must be done at the source. I am pleased that the World Health Organization itself indicates that it can manage for the coming six months with the 4 million that have already been pledged. The World Bank calls Congo potentially one of the richest economies on the continent. How is it possible that until recently not a single MPOX vaccine was available in Congo? I cannot answer this question. That also concerns the question of what the country itself is undertaking in response. I will just check my notepad again, Chair. I have no further questions.

Now it is being made to seem as if the majority of regular healthcare is entirely dependent on private equity firms. That is, of course, nonsense. Similarly, the opposite picture is being portrayed: that if we didn't have Co-Med, a great many people would be without a GP. A great many people are without a GP because Co-Med existed. If Co-Med hadn't existed, those people would simply have had a GP, you know. They would have simply had a GP who is involved in the daily affairs of a family and a patient, and who knows the patient inside and out. They would not have been dependent on freelancers providing care under the guise of private equity within a Co-Med structure, simply thinking they are fine with it. Moreover, those people are also referred to secondary care more often, because then they are done with it. All in all, I would say: if you simply have a normal, regular GP and not all sorts of strange constructions involving private equity firms taking over buildings and deploying GPs via freelance structures… I am just giving an example, mind you. Surely you wouldn't want that either, I say to the NSC.

You act as if I am not making every effort to get rid of that illegal stuff, but we certainly are. I just mentioned the international trail, which we are fully pursuing. The moment this is still being produced legally in EU countries — we have an open internal market — it will come here. It is apparently very popular here, because it is being used here for domestic disputes and by criminals in settling scores, with all the consequences that entails. I want to get rid of it as soon as possible. But there is a lot of effort between wanting and achieving. It is not automatic.

However, this motion is too assertive, and I advise against it. The motion by Mr. Stoffer regarding document no. 37 concerning the continuation of efforts in water management is a commendable motion. Water management is a key priority of this Cabinet's policy. In this regard, I will continue cooperation with the broad Dutch water sector, including companies, knowledge institutions, and civil society organizations. However, because I still need to develop my policy, I do want the scope to carefully examine the objectives regarding water. I therefore wish to review this comprehensively. For this reason, I advise against this motion. Mr. Stoffer had another motion, regarding document no. 38, concerning the GNI link. As stated, the Cabinet has decided not to update the ODA budget this summer. You cannot deduce future decision-making from this, neither in a positive nor a negative sense. We will take stock again in the spring. I cannot anticipate that at this time. The Prime Minister and the Minister of Finance have also indicated this. Therefore, I must advise against the motion. The motion by Mr. Ceder regarding document no. 39 concerns policy coherence and agricultural products in middle- and low-income countries. Within the WTO, developing countries have considerable scope for protecting their own agricultural sector. I would like to note that trade can also contribute to food security. It is important to pay attention within trade policy to the need for developing countries to build up their own production capacity. I will certainly take this into account. I can therefore give the House my judgment on this motion.

Yes. The motion regarding document no. 2133: advised against. The motion regarding document no. 2134: judgment of the House. The motion regarding document no. 2135: advised against. We are already actively working on removing paper and physical obstacles and a military Schengen area. This is therefore too great an infringement on sovereignty.

You know, at the COA and all other reception locations, they do their utmost to organize the reception as well as possible and under the best conditions. I have complete confidence in that. Of course, something will inevitably go wrong once in a while. I believe that too, but I cannot say that it is all bad or terrible. Those are not your words, but that is how I am summarizing what you are saying. I think they meet the requirements for a great many things. Of course, facilities in an emergency shelter are sometimes less well-equipped, but generally speaking, the facilities in many emergency shelters are good.

I think Mr. Bushoff knows me a little and knows that I also have to monitor the feasibility of everything already on my plate and execute it. I signed up for that, so I will do it. That is, of course, also the wish of the majority of the House, so I am going to do that. For the rest, I currently have too little overview of the big picture. I am going to fight for healthcare. I will always do that, and I have always done that. That is the only thing I can say up to now.

I see it differently. As Ms. Martens said earlier… I have a question about the risk. You haven't really answered that question, by the way: who dares to take that risk, us or them? There is simply a chance that those companies will leave. They said so, too. And then what? Then we have nothing. I think that would simply be a very bad scenario. We had to reach a compromise; this is what it became.

Chair. The PVV wants to significantly limit labor migration to the Netherlands. It is high time that our country regains control over the influx of foreign workers. This is not only the wish of the PVV, but of a great many Dutch people. The nuisance in our neighborhoods and the pressure on the housing market and social services can no longer be ignored. For too long, the burdens of labor migration have been shifted onto our society. We are therefore pleased that the coalition programme announces concrete steps to address the issue of labor migration. In addition, the PVV is pleased that the Minister is working on a productivity agenda to reduce dependency on low-paid migrant workers by innovating, deploying labor-saving technology, and working smarter in low-productivity sectors. But when can we expect this agenda, and how will the Minister prevent it from being hijacked or opposed by companies or sectors that are precisely dependent on low-paid migrant workers? The PVV urges the Cabinet to sit down with the provinces and jointly develop policies aimed at actively discouraging labor migration to low-productivity sectors. There are still provinces that directly or indirectly facilitate this labor migration, leading to additional pressure on our housing market, infrastructure, and social services. A joint approach is needed to improve this situation and put the interests of the Dutch population first once again. Last September, the PVV, together with the VVD, NSC, and BBB, submitted a motion requesting an increase in fines imposed by the Labor Inspectorate on companies that illegally employ foreign workers. This motion was held over at the time. Research shows that these fines have not been indexed since 2005, causing them to lose their deterrent effect. As a result, employing illegal immigrants has in many cases even become profitable. The PVV asks the Minister what steps have been taken in the meantime. This issue is not an isolated one. Unscrupulous employment agencies play a major role in facilitating exploitation and other abuses. Therefore, the PVV is pleased with the measures against these practices, as included in the coalition programme and the legislative proposal on the Admission of the Provision of Labour (Wtta). The PVV asks whether it is also possible to permanently bar individuals who have committed exploitation from this sector and to hold them jointly and severally liable, so that they are held personally responsible for their actions. This will send a clear signal that exploitation will not be tolerated. Furthermore, the Minister is working on a plan to utilize the untapped Dutch labour potential as an alternative to labour migration and to make work more rewarding. These are two points of great importance to the PVV. Working more hours could eliminate a large proportion of the open vacancies. Research shows that if part-time workers were to work an average of four extra hours per week, the labour shortage would be almost completely resolved. The PVV has high expectations of these plans but understands that the Minister needs time to implement them properly. Nevertheless, we call on the cabinet to quickly come up with concrete measures so that Dutch people who work more hours actually get more money in their own pockets, and the pressure on the labor market is relieved.

It is, of course, very tempting to respond to that, but I am not going to do so. Those are your words, your assumptions. I have invited you for a conversation several times. Unfortunately, it never came to that. I would also have liked to cycle to Maastricht — cycle, yes, certainly — to do it there. I haven't heard from you since. In the first week after the recess, I invited you to simply have a cup of coffee here in the House. I asked if an appointment could be made. That did not happen. Mr. Timmermans, I have respect for you as a politician, but for the sake of the purity of the debate, I would like to ask you to stop making personal attacks and cutting people down to size. That does not befit you. You have a distinguished record, and this does not do you credit.

Yes, Chair, just for a moment. This is about child poverty. Come on! It doesn't say here: abolish child poverty. That is actually what we should be doing. It doesn't even say: halve child poverty in the Netherlands. It says: keep a finger on the pulse regarding child poverty. And this Minister dares to say here: that is not up to me, I advise against the motion, you had better vote against it. This is unbelievable!

Many thanks, Chair. At the end of this week, Mr. Schoof will be able to take his seat at the tables of the Brussels summit for the first time as Prime Minister. We wish the Prime Minister every success, and we hope and believe that he will be able to do good business for the Netherlands there. Chair. A breath of fresh air is blowing not only in the Netherlands, but throughout Europe. The realization has now truly sunk in among many Europeans that the European external borders are too porous and that our continent needs to gain more control over who is allowed to come here and who is allowed to stay, for how long, and under what conditions. This week, the Prime Minister of Poland, also the former President of the European Council, Donald Tusk, advocated for a temporary total moratorium on asylum. The Hungarian Prime Minister, Viktor Orbán, said last week that he, like the Netherlands, desires an opt-out so that Hungary also gains the right to shape its own asylum policy. Austria and the Netherlands are joining forces and focusing on the punishment and effective return of illegal migrants. The Netherlands' idea to build so-called return hubs—places outside the EU where rejected asylum seekers stay until they return to their country of origin—is currently receiving a great deal of support from many colleagues of our own Minister Faber. Today it was announced that the President of the European Commission, Ms. Ursula von der Leyen, is also in favor of these return hubs. The citizens of France and Germany, for example, have suffered enormously in recent months from the violence and terror perpetrated by asylum migrants whose applications have often long since been rejected. The new French Minister of the Interior, Bruno Retailleau, wants to criminalize illegal residence, just as the PVV arranged in the framework agreement. But he goes even further and has even more ideas. For instance, he wants to be able to imprison criminal illegal migrants for three-quarters of a year without judicial intervention, a form of administrative detention. These and other developments demonstrate that European leaders are prepared to take real steps. As far as our group is concerned, this Brussels summit of EU heads of government therefore comes not a day too soon. The ball is on the proverbial spot for strict migration measures. In our estimation, therefore, our Prime Minister can kick this ball into the net on Thursday and Friday. I would like to ask Prime Minister Schoof, via the Speaker: could you indicate exactly what your position will be regarding this specific theme at the upcoming European summit? What proposals will our Prime Minister put forward to reduce the influx of asylum seekers and increase the outflow? What concrete results does the Prime Minister expect to return with? When will the Netherlands start to feel the effects of this at the earliest? Speaker. Naturally, there are other important themes on the agenda as well. For instance, the European leaders wish to discuss the EU’s competition policy and the turbulent world around us. Regarding the latter: it is of course always interesting to discuss foreign policy here in the House — I can imagine that it is no different for the Prime Minister during such a summit. But it must all lead to something, and above all, it must serve Dutch interests. It would therefore be good if the Member States themselves, in particular, were to shape their foreign policy. In many cases, that is also much more practical, given that it regularly appears that the interests of the various Member States diverge. We also observe divergent opinions here and there among the governments of the respective member states regarding the foreign policy to be pursued. With regard to the competitiveness of the European Union as a whole, we would like to remind our Prime Minister that he must always and everywhere—including during the upcoming summit—consider what is in the Dutch interest. Unlike many other EU economies, the Dutch economy is relatively robust and less dependent on, for example, the manufacturing industry, which is currently suffering so much from the productive power of countries like China. Let us, as the Netherlands, therefore be restrained in pursuing all kinds of centrally orchestrated European bailout plans. Before we know it, we will end up in a situation where Dutch money has to be used to keep the economies of our neighbouring countries afloat. I look forward to hearing the Prime Minister's view on all of this and await his response shortly. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

First of all, the note was not sent to the wrong address. It was simply meant for the European Commission and not the European Council in this case. That was also diplomatically supported, so otherwise we would have to say that our diplomatic service is inadequate, and I do not want to say that; I really do not want to go that far. So, thought was certainly given to who the note was sent to. That is the first point. Secondly, this was my first visit to Luxembourg. As everyone understands, it is of course highly unlikely that I would return immediately with an opt-out under my arm. An opt-out is a long-term process, a matter of years. It is not something that can be pulled off in a jiffy. It is true, of course, that the civil service apparatuses of several ministries are working on this opt-out. Consultations are also being held regarding it. Those are simply ongoing. But the strongest part of our message is that we want a stricter asylum policy in the Netherlands and in Europe. And that came across well.

I take issue with the remark that everyone — everyone, mind you! — is just going to let loose and go off the rails this year. The vast majority of people are simply standing on the street with their friends, family, or neighbors. They are just having fun setting off fireworks. They aren't going off the rails at all. It is such a small group that is going off the rails. Everything that small group does is already forbidden. Attacking emergency services personnel is forbidden, committing vandalism is forbidden, illegal fireworks are forbidden. Even if you have legal fireworks, it is all already forbidden. Throwing fireworks is already forbidden, setting them off too early is already forbidden. It is all already forbidden.

No. Again, I do not think so. I do not agree with what Mr. Bontenbal says. Nor do I blame the Prime Minister. I am merely saying: he could have known… It was not for nothing that we said we would vote last Thursday after the lunch break. That had to do with the European Council. It also had to do with the fact that the Prime Minister could have known how we would vote. I do not think that… Nor do I blame him for it. I am merely saying: you could have known that, Mr. Prime Minister. In my opinion, that is a fact. It is simply professional that we subsequently talk to each other: how do we get out of this and how do we give the Prime Minister… Again, I too want to give the Prime Minister maximum leeway now to conduct those negotiations. I have done that, all four of us have done that. We are not putting any obstacles in his way. We are just saying—which is very normal in a parliament—that when he returns, we will review it again and that we might support it, but that we might also not support it. That is just part of the process: first see what you get. Does it meet the requirements? Have the risks Mr. Eerdmans rightly mentions been reasonably eliminated, or have they been insufficiently remedied? If so, we are not in favor of it; otherwise, possibly we are. That is how it goes. The Prime Minister now has a free hand. No one is holding him back. He is not on a leash. He is as free as a bird, and we are as free as a bird to form our own opinion afterwards.

Thank you, Chair. I will now briefly summarize everything. It is incomprehensible that asylum seekers who can travel independently from Africa to the Netherlands are, once in the Netherlands, taken to the doctor by taxi at the expense of the Dutch taxpayer and driven throughout the country. Therefore, the following motion. The House, having heard the debate, noting that asylum seekers are transported throughout the country by taxi at the expense of the Dutch taxpayer; considering that asylum seekers can also travel by public transport; requests the government to cease free taxi transport for asylum seekers, and proceeds to the order of the day.

I am sorry, but I cannot help but give the same answer — I am happy to do so, mind you, because I have respect for the D66 colleague — as to Mr. Bontenbal's question, for it was the same question. That answer is again twofold. On the one hand, it is a democratic procedure that the legislature itself established in this House. You cannot say that the House has created legislation here that is undemocratic. At the very least, the House chose to do so itself, for it is a law, the Aliens Act, which, including Articles 110 and 111, has been ratified by this House and by the Senate like any law. So do not come to me claiming that it is undemocratic, because it is not. I do not know if your party voted for it, but it is simply a law that a majority of both Houses voted for. Otherwise, it would not have become law. The second reason is: there is a Continuation Act which, as I already mentioned, goes directly to the House of Representatives in accordance with Article 110, paragraph 2, of the Aliens Act. That Royal Decree therefore states: we are going to do this now. That Continuation Act must go to the House immediately, and if the House then says "we do not want that," it lapses immediately. The Prime Minister will then have to immediately put an end to the Royal Decree he issued and any implementing orders. That can be dealt with in the House immediately. I therefore dispute the claim that the House would not have a say. As soon as the House receives that Continuation Act, it can deal with it and vote on it within the same week. Then you will know within the same week whether it can proceed or not.

With your permission, Chair, I will now proceed to answer the question I received from Mr Thijssen regarding one-on-one business. I can reassure Mr Thijssen on this point, for in the usual course of diplomatic relations, Member States naturally hold normal meetings with one another, including for the promotion of trade. Competence regarding trade policy naturally rests with the Commission. There are absolutely no ambiguities regarding this. I hope to reassure Mr Thijssen with this.

Now, don't make it sound unattractive. But I will give a serious answer. I am talking about — I will come back to this later — convicted criminals who hold dual nationality. I believe that their nationality should be revoked if they have been convicted and if they hold multiple nationalities. That is already possible. As far as I am concerned, we should extend that to antisemitism. I would also like to see them indeed deported from the Netherlands in that case. That is not easy. That won't be arranged tomorrow. So, that does not apply to everyone. My message to Muslims in the Netherlands — I have said this before — is actually quite simple: if you abide by our rules, values, and laws, then as far as I am concerned, you are welcome to stay here. I mean that sincerely. You can achieve anything in the Netherlands. There are also many people within the Muslim community who work hard, build careers, study, and do absolutely nothing wrong. I feel no need whatsoever to ask those people to leave the Netherlands. But if you don't do that, if you start applying Sharia-like practices, such as antisemitism, honor killings, female genital mutilation, violence against homosexuals, and you name it, if you don't abide by our rules, then please leave. Leave. Leave voluntarily as far as I'm concerned, but leave. The difference between you and me, then, is that I want to limit myself to people who have committed a crime. Perhaps that is no difference; perhaps you think so too. I want to remove people from the Netherlands if they have committed a crime. Then I want to ask them to leave and even compel them to go. According to the law, they then qualify for denaturalization. Then I want to do that. I have no ambition whatsoever to get people out of the Netherlands who abide by the rules in this country and who are simply leading a good life here.

No, not at all. The point is the following. The Minister has now decided — and we as a party support this — to halt asylum decisions for all applications that have already been submitted. All new applications will not be processed yet either. Consequently, a number of the people whose applications have already been processed are now status holders. These people would like to bring their families over. But who says that there are no people in those families who were part of the regime? Surely they must also undergo the same check? Surely they must receive the same treatment as all other new applications?

I leave the House's judgment regarding the motion on document no. 126. I have a meeting with the NZa in any case and can include this in that. Then I come to the motion on document no. 127. That has been advised against. As is known, I cannot and may not interfere with collective labour agreement negotiations. I see that the collective labour agreement for pharmacy assistants is lagging behind. We do indeed have OVA funds, and that portion is indeed reserved for pharmacy assistants. I can point this out, but I cannot intervene. I am not permitted to do so.

I don't think we understand each other. You listed what you have already done. You dismissed suggestions from several Members of Parliament and made a promise to look into how you can collect outstanding money from fare dodgers. That is what I understood. Well, fine that you are doing that, but it doesn't help the people in Ter Apel at all. What are you going to do differently after today than what you have already rattled off? Perhaps you could say that again. Maybe I missed it. It must be me. But what are you going to do?

Thank you, Chair. Five years ago, the Dutch Red Cross started providing food aid — I repeat: food aid — in our own wealthy country. That is for working parents who are in debt and for people who call in a panic because there is only a carton of milk left in the fridge, but also for migrant workers who have nowhere to go. The Red Cross wonders whether it will ever be able to scale down this aid in the Netherlands. They say that the problems are growing and that the government really needs to come up with a solution. That is what we are here for. Billions for militarization are flying around us — there is money for that, enough even — while poverty in the Netherlands is becoming increasingly severe.

"That means adhering to the Constitution, laws, treaties, European and international law, and legal principles." That is the first point of the common baseline for safeguarding fundamental rights, which was necessary to bring about this cabinet and this coalition. Closing borders, preventing food, water, and medical supplies—which are currently in severe shortage in hospitals in Gaza—from entering the country, and withholding food, water, medical supplies, and other forms of humanitarian aid: these are war crimes. That cannot be denied, not even by you. What are you going to do? We can discuss here that you must condemn it. It is a disgrace that you are not doing so. That is just about the easiest thing to do. But there is also something else to do to exert pressure so that those borders open and water, food, medical supplies, and humanitarian aid can come that way again, and so that those war crimes can be tackled and corrected. These include, for example, sanctions and suspending the EU Association Agreement. You have a European Council. Are you going to advocate there to terminate that treaty?

Chair, thank you. I will begin with the questions. Mr. Bamenga asks whether I can inform the House of Representatives about the discretionary scope for humanitarian resources. I can assure Mr. Bamenga that I will include this point in my answers to the questions regarding the first supplementary budget. That will be submitted to your House shortly. So, I can promise you that. Ms. Dobbe asked where the weapons in the conflicts in the DRC and Sudan come from. She asked: how can you ensure that no weapons enter Sudan and the DRC from the Netherlands or via the Netherlands? It is indeed concerning that weapons are ending up there. The Cabinet shares those concerns. The Netherlands is discussing this worrying situation with regional partners and emphasizes the necessity of an end to the conflict and the importance of compliance with the UN arms embargo in Darfur. During the Council of State meeting last November, the Netherlands also drew the attention of Member States to the risk of weapons being diverted to Sudan. Chair. I will now turn to the motions.

Well, to be honest, I don't want to jump to conclusions on that either. We look at Eindhoven Airport. It is perfectly possible to do both there. I think there is a need for both in this country. We will see what we can do here at this airport.

I can give the House my opinion on the motion regarding document no. 1227, provided I may interpret it to mean that I will enter into dialogue with NS regarding the measures they are taking to improve the cleanliness of trains and that I will inform the House about this. If you say "you must interpret it differently," then I must unfortunately advise against it.

There has actually been good research, because that research showed that this pyramid scheme of just keep bringing people in does not work. The population will grow, grow, grow: it has to stop at some point. That is why we ask this Minister to present his productivity agenda to actually reduce the dependence on low-paid migrant workers, for example by innovating. We all know the asparagus harvesting machine currently being tested, which can relieve a lot of pressure. Labor-saving technology can be deployed, but also working smarter in low-paid sectors. These are all possibilities that we want to explore, that the Cabinet is exploring, whereby we actually become less dependent on low-paid migrant workers. Because it has to stop at some point. We cannot, whatever that advisory council said, just keep bringing people in. The Netherlands is only a small country. We have already reached our maximum. So we have to start thinking differently. We have to dare to do that. We have that courage. I trust that the Minister has this courage too. Then I will continue. The PVV has questions for the Minister regarding the feasibility of the mandatory disability insurance for the self-employed, as announced in the coalition programme. There is much criticism from the Tax and Customs Administration and the UWV concerning the complexity and feasibility, with assessments of "unfeasible" and "unfeasible, unless". The Tax and Customs Administration states that no effective date can be set, as the legislative proposal is unfeasible. The UWV indicates that implementation is in any case not possible before January 1, 2030, and possibly not even afterwards, unless there is an amendment to the broader disability system. The Minister indicated in his letter that he will study this immediately, but can he already give us an initial response? What is he likely to do? Although the mandatory disability insurance has been postponed, the PVV continues to advocate for an opt-out for self-employed individuals who can demonstrably prove they are sufficiently covered regarding their own disability risk. Self-employed individuals who cover their risk through private insurance, savings, or initiatives such as a mutual fund should have the option to opt out. What is the Minister's view on this? According to the Minister's letter regarding labor market policy, the Minister of Economic Affairs, together with the State Secretary for Public Transport and the Environment, is examining the consequences of trip logging for entrepreneurs and workers. Does the Minister of Social Affairs and Employment share the PVV's view that this measure causes a heavy regulatory burden for many entrepreneurs and workers? Is he prepared to advocate for the abolition of trip logging to reduce the regulatory burden on the labor market, as indicated in the coalition programme? To conclude: The PVV remains committed to advocating for significantly less labor migration to the Netherlands, making work more rewarding, and tackling fraudulent employment agencies and malicious self-employment. We have confidence in the measures mentioned in the framework agreement and expect the Cabinet to implement these plans quickly and effectively. The PVV will monitor and oversee this to ensure that these goals are actually achieved. Mr. Chairman, that concludes the first term.

I come to the motion on document no. 3281 by Member Van Zanten. That motion "requests the government to develop stricter frameworks to refuse or revoke asylum permits sooner when an irrevocable prison sentence has been imposed". That is the judgment of the House.

It is not the most severe measure. You are mistaken if you say that. I am therefore looking at the answers provided by the Minister of the Interior to Ms. Piri's questions. The Minister rightly states there: the most severe measure is Article 103 of the Constitution, which is a state of emergency. That is possible when there are special, extraordinary circumstances. But Articles 110 and 111 are also possible when there are—and I quote this—extraordinary circumstances that are not so serious that a state of emergency, within the meaning of Article 103 of the Constitution, must be declared, but which are serious enough that it is necessary to deviate from the Aliens Act. We did not include Articles 110 and 111 in the Aliens Act as a coalition; the legislature did. As far as I know, that was largely the CDA at the time. They chose that the legislature must have that option. We are using that option because the need is so high and we can no longer cope in the Netherlands. There are currently 60,000 to 70,000 people at the COA, including 20,000 who already have a permit. We are at our wits' end. The whole of the Netherlands sees that we can no longer handle it, so an instrument created by the legislature itself, which we are making use of, is much better than an emergency law.

It is true that the three points the State Secretary just mentioned have been postponed for a year. That is not what I am referring to. You know perfectly well that that is not what I am referring to. What you are saying now is correct. It has been postponed for a year, and that is why you are indeed settling for fewer hands at the bedside, with reduced staffing in elderly and nursing home care. It is a disgrace in itself that you intend to stand by that in 2026. That is true. But I am referring to the other cuts that you are not mentioning now, such as housing, living and care, and fall prevention. These are precisely the points included in the Dobbe amendment — I hope State Secretary Maeijer knows this, because both Minister Agema and State Secretary Maeijer voted for that amendment — and which you are not scrapping. That is my question. The amendment was submitted and you both agreed to it, yet you are not scrapping those points. Why not? It is simply a wish of the House to do so. You have not done it.
The professionals, the gynecologists, have opted for the Sittard-Geleen location in the event that staff shortages would not have been sufficiently averted after 2030.

I had a question from Ms. Slagt-Tichelman: do you receive a reward from the health insurer if you provide fewer hours, and a penalty if you provide more hours? In the context of my December letter, I inquired about this regarding palliative care with ZN and the health insurers. They do not recognize this bottleneck with respect to palliative care. There is no reward for providing fewer hours of care, nor is there a penalty if the number of hours is exceeded. Furthermore, it appears that at least four major health insurers remove palliative care from the benchmark and assess it separately. This means that it is not included in the overall efficiency agreements. Then, a question from Mr. Ceder regarding the suicide capsule. Like Mr. Ceder, I consider the use of a suicide capsule undesirable. The Cabinet specifically aims to reduce the number of suicides. In the Netherlands, it is a criminal offense to assist another person in committing suicide or to provide that person with the means to do so, should the suicide be successful. Providing a suicide capsule to someone who ends their own life with it falls under that criminal offense. Chair. Then a question from Ms. Paulusma regarding the safeguarding of knowledge, specifically in the training of medical specialists. As I also promised earlier in the debate, we will return in December to the implementation and elaboration of the previously submitted motion. This section will be included in that. Finally, the question from Mr. Thiadens regarding funding. As of 2025, this is included in the funding. The NZa is working on this, together with the parties. He is asking me somewhat too much when he asks exactly what that will look like. When more is known about what that will look like, I will inform your House. Then I will be finished with my questions.

I am very pleased with this question from Mr. Van Baarle. He speaks of a "mess". How did we get this mess? Through the policies of the past decades. That is the mess. That is where the mess originated. I am now the rubble remover. That is a completely different role.
I disagree with you. The cabinet is taking a great many measures to limit the influx. If we reduce that influx, then we will need fewer reception places. In my opinion, that is simply how it works.

I am not sneering at anyone, but I have a different view than Ms. Paulusma regarding this approach. Let’s be honest. If, at a time when the financial and economic situation in the Netherlands is significantly worse, you can still invest a net 4.3 billion euros in a lower deductible, then you are making a substantial contribution. DSW has calculated that 7% more patients will request referrals. These are people for whom financial reasons will pose less of a barrier. Does this solve everything? No. I cannot solve everything, but I consider the social hospital model—as I visited it at the BovenIJ hospital, and as it is being implemented at the hospital location in Heerlen—to be a very good example for situations where the doctor might get stuck with the patient because the patient has other problems on their mind. I think that is excellent. The people at the BovenIJ hospital in Amsterdam are very enthusiastic about this and the effect they are achieving. I think that is a good approach. My vision is: stop walking around with your symptoms; get referred to the hospital by your doctor if necessary. I promised Mr. Claassen to speak with Zuyderland about administrative time, and whether they can also be a frontrunner in that area. Who knows, I might still be able to persuade them to make agreements. Mr. Claassen also had a question about emergency posts, emergency clinics, and emergency plazas. What exactly is the difference between them all, I believe was the question he asked. You have an emergency department, the SEH, and for that, a quality framework applies that you will have to comply with. The staffing requirement is that you have at least one junior doctor with one year of clinical experience available 24/7. This requirement does not apply to all those other variants. Quality standards do apply, but not the one very stringent one that more and more hospitals are unable to meet. That is a staffing problem. I have discussed the social hospital, the quality framework, and maternity care as well. Anyway, if there are any further questions regarding Zuyderland, please let me know.

Sorry, Mr. Chairman. I will just continue. I believe I will reach Mr. Kent shortly. I left off at 1,800 crossings. It is an intensive network, one of the busiest networks in the world, which requires a great deal of maintenance. This maintenance is necessary because the rail system is under enormous pressure. Even though we are not yet quite at pre-corona figures, at peak times, when the rail system is truly challenged, the number of train passengers is already higher than ever. This has an impact on the performance of the rail system but also on the performance of the carriers, not only in the here and now but also for the coming years if we do not take action now. I have therefore taken my first major decision in close consultation with the Minister. We have had to allocate nearly 900 million euros in extra funding for the upkeep of the railway. We will continue to have to make difficult choices time and again in the future. We are going to do more maintenance in the coming years than ever before. To make smart and decisive choices regarding what is truly necessary and to keep costs under control, we have developed the Basic Rail Quality Level (BKN) with ProRail for the period starting in 2026. With the funding currently provided by this cabinet, a stable, long-term, and robust maintenance level for the Dutch railway network is secured. We are also already hard at work improving the railway. For instance, the renovation of Amsterdam Central Station is in full swing to enable increased capacity as part of the High-Frequency Rail Transport Program. This also ensures greater capacity for international train services to London and Paris. Work has also started on the railway curve at Meteren, specifically the Den Bosch-Vught section, including the construction of a temporary track in Vught to enable the construction of the deepened section there. This is intended to create a branch line from the Betuwe Line, allowing more freight trains to travel south via the Betuwe Route and relieving pressure on part of the Brabant Route. The first spade will finally go into the ground for the Maaslijn in the coming months as well. Mr. Chair. This is how we are putting the basics in order so that we can meet demand in the future as well. Naturally, you will notice this as a traveler. Train cancellations or delays are never pleasant news. However, we are doing everything we can to ensure this proceeds without too much inconvenience. Having the basics in order is one thing. Affordability is another point of attention. I have heard legitimate concerns about this from several Members of Parliament here. I will not mention their names, but almost everyone had something to contribute on this. Both Nibud and KiM have recently conducted research into the affordability of mobility. The KiM study shows that 10% of adult residents of the Netherlands experience problems paying for transport. Nibud even states that it is difficult for families with incomes up to and including the median income level to afford the journey they wish to make. That is a worrying development. That is why we also made basic security a point in the coalition programme. In my role as State Secretary, I am doing what I can. By this, I mean to say that ensuring good and affordable mobility is a joint undertaking. It is a task for ministries, provinces, municipalities, transport operators, and employers as well. In any case, I am pleased that I was able to do something about the price of the NS train ticket shortly after taking office. For the coming year, we have been able to halve the expected price increase of 12% to an increase of 6%, whereby we have been able to distribute the costs equally and NS is therefore also making a contribution. That is indeed not 0%; a number of parties indicate that. However, it is the substantial reduction that we all had in mind. It is the maximum achievable result, I also say to Mr. De Hoop. I also want to be clear. To implement no fare increase at all in 2025, nearly 200 million per year is required on a structural basis. That money is not available in the budget. I say this in advance as a preview of my response to the amendments submitted regarding this matter.

I would also prefer the PBL. However, the problem is that I have been required to do it in stages. I have to provide for those stages. But I certainly want to investigate further whether we can simply place that VBL there. We could perhaps also look into whether that might be possible at another location. But that is subject to change, because I am not certain. We need to investigate that further, but I am certainly open to it. After all, I also want these people to remain on the site. Ideally, I would like to send them back to their home country immediately.

Well, I also have a question for the Minister. I hope to receive a commitment to at least have it investigated whether a cap can be introduced. What we definitely shouldn't do... Look, the possibility exists. I have an opinion on that anyway. The PVV certainly has an opinion on the fact that we have interest rates that can rise to 60% per year. I find that truly abnormal. In any case, people should not be knowingly redirected via the Financing Guide, so that they think they are ending up at a company where this is not the case. Let's start there and then see how we can tackle this further. If you have been rejected by a major bank, for whatever reason, you are often already backed into a corner. People may not be well-informed enough about this. They think they are being referred correctly, and then they just walk into a sort of interest rate trap. We are thinking of more transparency and clarity, and no accumulation of interest rates. Since the debate, my mailbox has been flooded. I have received all sorts of very clear examples, with full names, from people who thought they had secured proper financing from such an institution. In the event of non-payment, the entire amount can be demanded after just five days, and there is a surcharge of 2.5%. People simply cannot figure it out anymore and are then forced to sell their business and their assets. So, more transparency, more clarity, and stricter criteria are needed. The moment they end up at the FinancieringsGids, I believe it should be monitored how things proceed from there.

Mr. Heutink said, I believe, "26 consecutive shifts." That cannot be true. It cannot be possible in the Netherlands, with our Working Hours Act and the way we intend to tackle this together, for this to happen. So: yes, of course I am going to take this up. Absolutely. I can promise that.

I see some similarity with the motion I just submitted on behalf of GroenLinks-PvdA, DENK, and the SP. I think we want the same thing, but you take a slightly different turn at the end. Namely, you request the government to map out how much it would cost if you were to do this. So, essentially, the question is: what would it cost to do what is stated in the joint motion by the SP, GroenLinks-PvdA, and DENK? Isn't that actually just a factual request for information? Why can't that simply be done during a factual question round regarding the budget? In fact, we know what it costs. I have calculated it before. You can do that just like that. It lies between 50 and 75 million. Then we receive that message from the government. And then what? That is the question for NSC. What are we going to do then?

I understand the question. It is of course difficult, but the Netherlands must abide by the ruling. Under the ruling, the resumption of deliveries to Israel is only permitted if there is a regional war and if Israel guarantees that it will not be used in Gaza. The Netherlands must therefore comply with that ruling. We cannot interpret it any other way.

Excuse me, Madam Chair. My point is that I find it regrettable that this is being presented in such black-and-white terms. The State Secretary is primarily participating in this and is taking the lead. I do not see it in such black-and-white terms. Everyone is entitled to their opinion—they may say "that Ellian has gone mad"—but I believe that the safety of the staff can be guaranteed. I do not think it will be pleasant for them. I acknowledge that immediately. I believe that this decision can still be avoided. But it is being presented in absolute terms. Entire departments are standing empty. Renovations are being carried out that make me really wonder... Yes, fire safety is at stake. If fire safety is at stake, then naturally you have to look into it closely. But is that because a power outlet isn't working somewhere, or is that because there are serious risks? I believe there are so many other things you can weigh up. So, I believe that ultimately it comes down to a choice. It is being presented very much in absolute terms, but it is a choice to see if we can arrange that detention boat. Maybe we can come up with something after all. We're going to look in Zaanstad. That's a correctional facility with more than 1,000 beds. We'll remove the ISD department and then ...

You say: we need to draw up proposals for that then. I have proposals ready, and I know that Mr. Bushoff has proposals ready. I am fine with that. We can arrange and do that. That is good. Then we will put it in the form of amendments. A minister cannot ignore those. Then it simply becomes law — it has to — and then we will get it done. But that is not the way of working, Mr. Claassen. Let me just remind you of the debate we had just had regarding the Zuyderland Hospital. Are we going to do this for all those subjects where we get a minister against us who fails to implement adopted motions time and again? Is that the way this party thinks it governs and expects to get something done in a coalition? Are we going to do that time and time again? Because then we don't need a minister at all. She might as well leave, and we can do it ourselves.

Yes, it is getting late. Everyone knows that climate policy is not my hobby, but when asked if I am committed to it, I say: certainly. We are all committed to it. The Cabinet speaks with one voice, and you can see the harmony in which we sit here. We work well and willingly together. I can tell you that the manners and atmosphere within the Cabinet are excellent. Then a question from Member Rooderkerk and Ms. Koekkoek: how is the mobility sector doing? That is certainly a small success story. In the past, an electric car might have been a left-wing, expensive hobby. By now, the Netherlands is truly at the forefront when it comes to the electrification of the mobility sector. There is still a great deal to be achieved in the trucking sector — that is actually just the beginning — but the Netherlands has already achieved a tremendous amount. If you disregard the buffer, we only need to deliver another 0.8 megatons to reach the target. So that is very good news. There are also numerous possibilities to achieve that goal. We are looking into those as well. Unfortunately, I must refer to the Spring Memorandum, as that is where we discuss money. Therefore, I will not be able to go into too much detail on that now. However, providing extra incentives for electric vehicles is naturally part of that. If necessary, you could also deploy additional biofuels if needed. But the Netherlands is doing incredibly well. We are one of the best countries in Europe when it comes to electrification. The good news is also that these cars are becoming increasingly better, cheaper, and accessible to more and more people. That is absolutely wonderful, because we do want this to remain affordable. If it can be sustainable and affordable, that is of course a real win-win situation. I am very positive about that. With the return of the truck toll, through the AanZET program, we want to roll out charging hubs so that the trucking sector can also experience this electrification in the coming years. What choices is the Cabinet making regarding the pricing of "the sacred cow"? That is not a question for LVVN; I will answer it, because this refers to cars, not cows. The Cabinet has indicated in the coalition programme that it will look into car taxes. We will also address this in the Spring Memorandum. You will be informed about this. Member Grinwis asks regarding the mitigation policy within the adaptation policy: are they aligned? Yes, in itself that is a very good question for Grinwis. The spatial alignment of mitigation policy and adaptation policy takes place in the partial revision of the North Sea Programme. In this revision, spatial considerations are made regarding both mitigation and adaptation. Not only wind turbines, but also sand extraction plays a role in this. In accordance with the coalition programme, the programme will be adopted this year and subsequently revised starting in 2028. But in itself, it is a good question. We are therefore taking it into account.

Mr. Ceder has already indicated this: it is stated in a council proposal. My information is that it is possible. I can accommodate you by stating in writing that this is possible from the COA's perspective. Perhaps I can help you with that. After all, my information is that it is possible.

I understand Ms. Teunissen's remarks. The problem is as follows. These people live in country A — let's call it country A for now; that is easy — where they are in a threatening situation because of their sexual preference. Suppose they have a partner there. The first person comes to the Netherlands and applies for asylum. It is very difficult to demonstrate that these people have a lasting relationship in such a country. You do end up with a certain inequality. I admit that, because this couple might well have wanted to get married, but they were not given the opportunity in their home country. So then you end up with an unequal situation. You actually don't want an unequal situation. But the fact remains that these people, the moment they have a serious relationship, must also be able to demonstrate that, and that is difficult if you come from such a country A. At least, I assume you find that difficult too, if I may make that assumption. That is a difficult situation. Of course, the IND will always continue to look at specific cases. This partner can, of course, also apply for asylum themselves. That possibility remains open. The moment this partner applies for asylum themselves, the chance that that asylum request will be granted is, of course, also very high.

I will leave the Minister's weak remarks aside for a moment. The point we are making here, of course, is that a very large part of the world, including the BRICS countries, effectively does not recognize the Criminal Court. That is never going to happen either. We are naturally opposed to international law; the Minister knows that. Isn't it, then, a much more logical path to recognize it and simply stop doing it altogether? My question is as follows. In effect, Europe—it is not even the West anymore, since America wants nothing to do with it anymore—is imposing its will on the rest of the world through the International Criminal Court. Isn't that very serious? I call that imperialism, and I am certainly opposed to that.
Oh, you were going to have cocktails with me too, but it just never happens.

After all, we have previously seen the PVV lash out fiercely at the State Secretary when it came to getting convicted criminals into prison. But if you say that we are going to issue fewer summary judgments, it means that more cases will become time-barred, that more cases will end up with a dismissal, and that criminals will therefore end up without punishment. This is the view expressed by various people within the criminal justice system. The PVV's claim that it has the strictest policy ever against crime has certainly fallen flat on its face today.

That is absolutely not the case. I want to state this through the Chairman. We simply looked at the interim ruling of the International Criminal Court. We examined: is Israel currently complying with those rulings? One of the components of this is that Israel must provide humanitarian aid. Well, Israel supplies 6,000 truckloads of food per month. 6,000! Mr. Van Baarle could have known that as well, because COGAT spoke in the Dutch Parliament and explained how much they do. What is also not being mentioned, for example, is that Israel supplied 2.5 million polio vaccines to combat the polio epidemic in Gaza. If you simply look at that ruling, Israel is doing everything in this war to keep records of everything, to fulfill all legal obligations, to report to the International Criminal Court, and to meet all those conditions. We are simply adhering to international law, as we signed it in the Declaration of the Rule of Law.

But that is the essence of this amendment. I have extended a hand. I think that Mr. El Abassi, with the best will in the world… My civil service provides advice. There are many amendments before us. If it is possible, the judgment rests with the House, but if it is advised against, there is definitely something wrong the moment it *is* adopted. That brings me to the section on fraud and embezzlement. If there is one thing that unites the House and all of us, and that everyone finds unacceptable, it is embezzlement from the healthcare budget. We do not know exactly how much money is involved, but we do know that every estimate comes to a very high figure. Whether that embezzlement from the healthcare budget occurs legally, through fraud, or even by criminal organizations: we must do everything in our power to keep this out of healthcare. We will do this starting in 2025 with the Act on Promoting Cooperation and Legitimate Care. It will then become easier for agencies involved in tackling fraud to exchange information via the Healthcare Fraud Information Hub and compare it with each other's information. I am therefore pleased that two weeks ago I was able to sign the deed of incorporation for the Healthcare Fraud Information Hub to provide the legal basis. This law also provides for the so-called Warning Register, enabling municipalities and health insurers to warn each other about fraudsters. Through the Act on the Integrity of Business Operations for Healthcare and Youth Care Providers (Wibz), I will set conditions under which profits may be distributed in the healthcare sector. In doing so, I am making it more difficult for profiteers in the healthcare sector. As an ultimate sanction, the bill offers the possibility of revoking licenses. A standard will also be introduced to promote greater governance integrity. Finally, the Dutch Healthcare Authority will be given a strong coordinating role in combating healthcare fraud. With this package, I am narrowing the loopholes. I do not have the confidence that I can eradicate healthcare fraud entirely, but we are going to make an extra attempt.

Mr. Bevers asks what is being done regarding accessibility in the new Parliament building. Every government building must comply with existing legislation and regulations regarding accessibility. When government buildings are renovated or newly constructed, the level of the Integral Accessibility Standard is applied as a norm in addition to legislation and regulations, ensuring that government offices become increasingly accessible. When adapting a monumental building, such as the Binnenhof, regulations concerning the protection of monuments must also be taken into account. The accessibility standard is therefore applied as much as possible. On July 6, 2022, the Presidium approved the final design, which explains how integral accessibility is being realized and also where no interventions are being made. The publicly accessible areas will be integrally accessible. An extensive package of measures has also been defined for the non-public areas, drawn up after consultation with the consultancy firm Ongehinderd. Furthermore, discussions were held with experts by experience within the House of Representatives regarding integral accessibility, including members of the Construction Supervision Committee.

Good evening. First of all, thanks to Mr. De Hoop and his team for submitting this bill. I believe it is absolutely necessary that we have this debate today. We have also been in a rather absurd situation since last week. I would first like to weigh the coalition's proposals against the bill before us today. Last Wednesday, the first news regarding the Spring Memorandum leaked out. At that moment, I was genuinely delighted. Geert Wilders announced that the rent increases for social housing for 2025 and 2026 would not go ahead and that a billion euros had been reserved for a grocery bonus. Those are very justified measures. Many tens of thousands of tenants have also taken action for this. We all know that after the leak comes the bitter end. But this bitter? Let me start with the so-called grocery bonus. The way the Cabinet is doing it, the rent freeze actually generates money. Until 2030, it will need to spend approximately 2.5 billion less on housing allowance. That 2.5 billion has therefore already been reserved for tenants in the budget. Of that 2.5 billion, the cabinet is going to give 1 billion back to tenants for a so-called grocery bonus. They are pocketing approximately 400 million themselves. Money reserved for tenants is therefore disappearing into the cabinet's pockets, yet you present it as a bonus. Isn't that simply political fraud? That is what we ask the cabinet and the coalition parties. You are making tenants pay for their own bonus and, in doing so, are swindling them out of their pockets, yet then presenting it as a gift. That is political fraud and a con trick at the expense of people who are struggling more and more to pay for groceries. Surely this is inexplicable? Mr. Chairman. However pleased we are with the announced rent freeze for two years, it became very bitter when it turned out that while a portion of tenants are rightfully receiving a rent freeze, rents for another portion of tenants continue to rise. In 2025, rents will rise by 4.1% in the private sector and by no less than 7.7% for mid-market rental housing. Surely this is inexplicable? On December 19, NSC and PVV said: "The proposed maximum mid-market rent increase of 7.7% is unacceptable." Those are your words, I say to NSC and PVV. But however unacceptable it may be, it is going ahead anyway with the coalition's new plans. One tenant gets a rent freeze and another gets a rent increase of 7.7%. That is inexplicable. The cabinet is pitting tenants against each other. I would like a response. But unfortunately, it is going to get even worse, because the coalition also wants to allow much higher rents in the near future and amend the Affordable Rent Act. Smaller landlords will no longer be required to comply with the Affordable Rent Act. Fewer homes will then fall under the law, causing them to become part of the expensive rental market. Homes in the major cities, in particular, may be rented out at higher prices. Dutch rents are already among the highest in Europe. This coalition wants to raise them even further. Surely that is inexplicable, especially to NSC and PVV? Less than a week ago, they blocked exactly this plan. NSC MP Welzijn accused the Minister at the time of stirring up unrest. PVV MP Mooiman said it was "unwise to allow even higher rents; rents should actually go down." Now they are doing it themselves. Surely this is inexplicable? Mr. Chair. While private landlords are being appeased with the Spring Budget Agreement, housing corporations are being squeezed dry. They will soon receive less income. That is very bad news. Fewer homes can be improved. As a result, tenants remain stuck in homes that cause illness and mold. As a result, the energy bills of many tenants remain high. Above all, far fewer homes can be built. It is inexplicable that there is hardly any real compensation for the corporations in the Spring Budget Agreement. It is inexplicable that corporations, and therefore tenants, still have to pay corporate income tax and ATAD, a tax for tax-avoiding multinationals. Much also remains unclear with the Spring Budget package. We also ask the Cabinet whether that justified rent freeze actually applies to all social tenants. Or does that only apply to social tenants renting from housing corporations? Does this also apply to social tenants who have to pay an income-dependent rent increase? Chair. The consequence of this package of measures is an even deeper housing crisis. A monstrosity has emerged from the negotiations, an incoherent package full of contradictions. The choice was made for poll-driven panic politics instead of solutions for the housing crisis. If you are looking for a social rental home, want an alternative to an overpriced rental home, or are living in a home full of mold that is causing illness, you are simply screwed with this plan. In the short term, this saves the coalition; in the longer term, we are left with an ever-deepening housing crisis. Chair. Weighing this against the De Hoop Act before us today, we see that De Hoop is indeed presenting a package that offers coherence, is good for tenants, and does not further deepen the housing crisis. We will therefore wholeheartedly support that Act. The rent freeze is a necessity, not only for social tenants but for all tenants. It is good that this law not only regulates that, but also ensures that the construction and improvement of homes continue by scrapping corporate income tax and ATAD. My party has always said that it is antisocial to charge our tenants corporate income tax and a tax intended for tax-avoiding multinationals. We do have concerns about the timing, however. May 1st is the statutory deadline by which housing corporations must announce rent increases or freezes, but the Senate is already in recess. Wouldn't it be wiser, just as in 2021, to arrange the rent freeze for social tenants or the rent increase taking effect on July 1st by means of a motion? Mr. Chairman, for the SP it is clear: we can only solve this housing crisis if we do not view housing as a market. We have also launched a campaign for this, *Wonen zonder winst* (Housing without profit), which is already supported by 25,000 people. We want an additional 1 million affordable homes, a rent freeze for all tenants, and tenant participation. We will continue with this in the coming period. Thank you, Chairman.

Naturally. I agree with the motion on document no. 2157: to speak out against the abolition of the veto right in all European negotiations. Advised against. Much decision-making already takes place with qualified majorities. The motion on document no. 2158 concerns VDL Nedcar. Untimely. That does not belong in this debate, but must be included in a debate with the Minister of Economic Affairs and the Minister of Defence, respectively.

I don't drive a car into a wall at all. That is the first thing. Secondly, the IND will simply remain at full strength for the next two years. I also reiterate that the upcoming package of measures must start bearing fruit.

First, let me briefly address this point, as it concerns only a minor matter: the failure to implement an adopted parliamentary motion. However, I do have a question about that. I am beginning to worry a little about this Prime Minister's sense of democracy. After all, the cabinet is still discussing and considering the course of action regarding the asylum crisis and bypassing parliament, despite the fact that the Senate has been clear. So, we are now receiving confirmation. While the majority of the House—including the PVV and NSC—was also clear during the first term that the adopted motion must be implemented, the Prime Minister is repeating arguments that were already presented in the answers to the factual questions. He is essentially saying: it is complex and unclear, so you just do it yourselves. Well, that is a fine mess. Didn't we adopt a motion here that, if I am not mistaken, says "requests the government"? It does not say: let's see what the parliamentary majority says and then we will take up the challenge ourselves. The Prime Minister has heard the parliamentary groups of the PVV and NSC, as well as a number of other groups. I therefore put the question to the Prime Minister once again: why is he not implementing that adopted parliamentary motion? I do not want a repetition of the arguments just given for that. But please, at the very least, show the democratic awareness that you cannot say: I am not going to implement that adopted parliamentary motion myself; just do it yourselves. I know a few more examples like that.

The motion regarding document no. 503, also submitted by Mr. Eerdmans, requests the government to explicitly take into account the exceptional nature of classic cars when developing environmental policy, and to recognize and treat them as sustainable mobile heritage. That is a commendable motion. We are already in discussions with the rolling heritage sector. They have asked us to develop a plan for this. We are going to do so with them.
