In that sense, I find the motion superfluous, but certainly commendable. I agree that our rolling heritage must be preserved. I always enjoy seeing such an older car. They are generally more polluting, though. Sometimes they are converted, but often they are more polluting. So, I would say we should preferably not use them for daily commuting. But it is nice to still see them on the streets, so I do think it is commendable. We are in discussions.

I stand up for Jewish Netherlands. I do not accept that, for whatever reason, Jews are being persecuted, that Jews are being hunted down in our capital. I never accept that. I accept that from no one. I will always call it out and I will always stand up for those people. That means you also have to indicate who did it. It is clear from all the footage, all the eyewitness accounts, all the videos, and everyone who experienced it, that they were all Muslims. They were largely Moroccans. There were no Swedes or Canadians among them. You have to dare to name that honestly, because we have had these problems in the Netherlands for decades. Every Dutch person recognizes this from home, at the market, at the fair, in the cinema, on a late-night shopping evening, or on the tram. Every Dutch person recognizes the problems that have reached us up to here by now, right here! I no longer accept that and I name it sharply, because I want a sharp solution. I will always continue to do so.

Certainly. But I will not let my people be insulted. Nor will I let my people be wrongly called racists. I will not let my people who said "we shouldn't have used the word 'population replacement'" be called Nazis after all. So of course, you are right, we are investing in that and we must invest in that. And I also want this cabinet to be a great success. I also want us to work out those plans from the framework agreement. But I will always, always, defend our ministers, our members of the House of Representatives, our members of the Senate, our voters, who have also often been called racists, and wrongly so.

That took some work with the motions on documents nos. 2230 and 2231, I can assure you. The motion on document no. 2230 can receive the House’s judgment, because, as stated in last week’s letter to Parliament, the Netherlands will remain opposed to eurobonds. The motion is therefore in line with this letter and consequently receives the House’s judgment. We will strictly monitor the budgetary rules. This is always the position of the Netherlands. I would like to request that the motion on document no. 2231 be deferred. As far as I am concerned, it is premature, because the policy brief regarding the MFF has yet to be sent to the House. If it is not deemed premature, it is advised against.

No. I share the concern. It is certainly not the intention to miss a year. However, given the process and the thoroughness involved, I really cannot promise that this will be available in the short term. And then, unfortunately, I have to advise against it.

No, but if it had been addressed to me, I would have advised against it. The motion regarding document no. 3331 receives the House's judgment. That brings us to the motion regarding document no. 3332 by Ms. Vondeling. I certainly understand this motion, but it comes too soon. I am dealing with the decision and departure moratorium. My request is to postpone this motion, because as soon as that moratorium is lifted, we will certainly look into it. If the opportunity arises, the Syrians simply must return home.

I think Ms. Kamminga is referring to the European CSDDD Directive. That has entered into force. We now have two years to transpose it into national legislation. So far, implementation is proceeding according to plan. The online consultation will start soon; I believe it was this week already. Perhaps it has just started. In any case, it is within this period. The guiding principle for the implementation is to implement the directive in a burden-reducing manner, in accordance with the cabinet's policy, meaning without national additions. That is exactly how we are going to do it.

Mr. Grinwis must add that the BDU is part of the SPUK. That is simply the reality. Therefore, these are not two separate arguments. The Cabinet did not state all at once that it is deliberately going to scrap the BDU. There is simply a general reduction of 10% on the SPUK funds. And yes, the BDU falls under that. Mr. Grinwis just asked me all kinds of questions. Those are justified, I say to him, but he should not be asking them of me. Mr. Grinwis should address these questions to the Cabinet. Why do I refer to the Cabinet? The reason for that is very simple. The Cabinet has already indicated that it will evaluate the effects of the reduction on the BDU and the SPUK funds. I think it would be very good if Mr. Grinwis were to ask the Cabinet how long that will take. Perhaps I can do that for him now. How long will that take, I ask the Cabinet. That is the right route. We could all start pushing things in between now, but once again, we don't have a tree on our backs, I say to Mr. Grinwis. We have made political choices. We are defending them here. That is our role. That is how we take responsibility. But to Mr. Grinwis, I say that he should address the Cabinet regarding the evaluation of the reduction in specific benefits.

I want to emphasize this one more time: previously, a homeless person in Venlo was chopped to pieces by a corn harvester because he was sleeping there. Look, I think everything you are saying now is all well and good, but there are people for whom this applies *now*. In the past few weeks, we have talked about crisis and emergency measures; there is an emergency *now*. People are homeless *now* and sleeping outside *now*. They are getting wet *now* and being exploited and discarded *now*. I want to ask the Minister the following. If he provides funding to municipalities to arrange shelter and if he believes this is inappropriate, why doesn't this Minister create a plan *now* to get all the people sleeping on the street off the street, offer them immediate shelter, ask where they come from and who their last employer was, and make that employer bear the costs? This revenue model really has to stop! I expect something more from the Minister than just saying that things have already been set in motion. It is happening *now*. This concerns the vast majority of migrants, who are brought to the Netherlands to be exploited and discarded. And you just said yourself that it involves thousands of people. Meanwhile, half of all people sleeping on the streets and homeless are migrant workers. The question, therefore, is what you are doing *now* to place the bill where it belongs, namely with the people who get rich from the inhumane situations of others.

The latter is at least a good step. I would say that the Minister should do that himself towards the surviving relatives. But I still do not understand why NSC is so reluctant regarding that compensation. We can simply acknowledge together that we have a debt of honor. Mistakes were made there. The Sorgdrager Commission has been very clear about that. Then we, as the Netherlands, also have the duty to simply give full compensation to the people who suffered and are still suffering because of that bombing. Why then do eleven people have to come here and go to court in the Netherlands to achieve that? Isn't that scandalous? Why don't we say *now*: those people will be compensated?

Thank you, Chair. The PVV party group has two motions to present. The House, having heard the debate, noting that Ukrainians working in the Netherlands for a Dutch employer contribute to our healthcare system only via the income-dependent contribution (Zvw); considering that the income-dependent contribution is disproportionate to the healthcare premiums paid by Dutch citizens; requests the government to require Ukrainians working in the Netherlands and receiving wages according to Dutch standards to start paying the basic premium, like everyone else, and proceeds to the order of the day.

I have always been teased a bit for saying that animals were here even before us. Whether you believe in the theory of evolution or the creation story, animals were here before humans. We were allowed to live with the animals. It is very strange that people were given rights at some point. I was always told: yes, if you give someone rights, then that person must also fulfill duties. That is bullshit, of course. A child or someone who is mentally incapacitated, for example someone who is mentally handicapped, often cannot fulfill duties either. So why wouldn't you do the same with animals? That is a question I have been asking for a very long time. It must have happened eventually, you would think. But sometimes I start to give up hope. The House, having heard the deliberations, requests the government to institute a trade and keeping ban on animals that may not be bred in the Netherlands, and proceeds to the order of the day.

We will certainly continue fighting for the hospital and everything else. But I stand by this: if tomorrow or the day after my government ministers, the PVV ministers, or Members of Parliament are once again wrongly portrayed as racists or whatever else, then I will do exactly the same thing again, for as long as I am walking around here.

Fine, Chair. I will start with a general point. Next comes the background. After all, the question was asked what exactly the problem is. After that, I will address the consequences and how we view other levels of government. That is how I will approach it. First, a general point. The Netherlands naturally still has the goal of having a circular economy by 2050. That is the aspiration we all share. I also believe that this is a necessity. In doing so, we are trying to use raw materials and products more efficiently and intelligently. Think of reuse, but also, for example, repair and recycling. The Circular Materials Plan will play a very important role in achieving this goal. It does indeed follow the National Waste Management Plan. That is not just a cosmetic adjustment. There is a real difference between them. Ultimately, this will ensure a clear overview of the national rules for waste management and a uniform approach by the various competent authorities. Mr. Buijsse asked what the major differences are. I will abbreviate it from now on, Chair: the CMP addresses all phases of the production chain, from design, production, and use up to and including waste processing and new applications of materials and products. It is therefore much more comprehensive than just the LAP. That focused solely on the waste component. Thus, the CMP will help companies make more circular choices and also support government authorities in decision-making, supervision, and enforcement. You just mentioned the date of January 8, the date on which we informed the House about the draft CMP and the start of the public consultation. This will run until February 18. During the consultation, everyone will have the opportunity to express their opinion before the CMP is ultimately adopted definitively. The views submitted will be accompanied by a response in a response note. Where necessary, the CMP will be adjusted accordingly. The final CMP, together with the response note, will be published at the end of 2025. Parallel to the drafting of the CMP, two legislative processes are also being completed: an amendment to the Environmental Management Act and to the related Orders in Council. Although the LAP and later the CMP are not legislation themselves, they do influence the decision-making of the competent authority regarding decisions on waste. Consider the issuance of permits, handling of waste, and the drafting of local regulations in this area. Article 10.14 of the Environmental Management Act stipulates that the competent authority must take the LAP into account. This means that the competent authority must generally follow the LAP, but may deviate from it in exceptional cases with sufficient justification. This will be no different with the CMP. With the present legislative proposal, two amendments are being implemented in the Environmental Management Act. First, the name change from LAP to CMP; this will be enshrined in legislation. Second, the statutory obligation that we are introducing for competent authorities to inform the Ministry if they deviate from the CMP, also known as the obligation to provide information — the difficult word we just discussed; I don't have an abbreviation yet, but I will try not to stumble over it. This obligation was already included in the LAP, I say to Ms. Wingelaar, but it actually lacked a legal basis, which meant that the obligation was not followed. Through the statutory obligation to provide this information, the Ministry gains more insight into where the CMP and practice may not align well. These reports can therefore ultimately provide us with valuable information regarding the obstacles companies experience. Based on these findings, it can be considered — I say clearly: can be considered — to amend the CMP so that the regulations better align with the needs of companies, which will help them realize their circular ambitions. Of course, I also heard clearly what Ms. Wingelaar said about her amendments, so I am going to attempt to convince her somewhat of the usefulness and necessity of the bill. I listened very carefully to the questions regarding cost capacity and burdens. I also heard support for the importance of the information with a view to ensuring uniformity and developing policy to stimulate the circular economy. I will make an attempt in a moment. I naturally hope that Ms. Wingelaar will then conclude that her amendment might be superfluous. But ultimately, that choice is of course up to Ms. Wingelaar herself. Ms. Wingelaar of the NSC and Mr. Boutkan of the PVV ask what the actual problem is. What signals are there regarding deviations? Has this ever been asked of the competent authorities? Do they also experience a barrier to being able to deviate? I will try to address all these questions. The LAP is flexible, due to taking into account everything laid down in the law. However, it is not intended that the existing deviation procedure be handled flexibly. The core principle is that the competent authority must always follow the procedure. That applies now as well. There are currently a handful of known signals regarding situations in which deviations are occurring. The evaluation of the LAP showed that many competent authorities regard deviating from the LAP as a kind of freedom that the LAP offers them. This theme was also discussed in the consultations we held with the LAP Steering Committee, in which the competent authorities are represented. What we are actually trying to do with this legislative proposal is to provide clarity. Moreover, deviating from the LAP is the exception, not the rule. We therefore foresee no obstacles. Should there be any, we will naturally include them in the evaluation of the obligation to provide. I then had a second question from Ms. Wingelaar regarding the consequences of not following the obligation to provide. How will compliance with that obligation be enforced? If an administrative body deviates from the CMP when making a decision and fails to inform the Minister—although "the State Secretary" is stated here—about this, then that administrative body is essentially acting in violation of the law. The memorandum in response to the report, which you received on September 2, went into more detail regarding the deviation procedure as described in the CMP. However, an error has crept into the answer. I must also take the blame for this. We can correct the error herewith. It is incorrect that a Minister can issue a directive. Nor is there any question of a declaration of no objection. The Minister can, however, provide advice regarding the intended deviation. Ultimately, failure by an interested party to comply with the obligation to provide information can lead to the annulment of the intended decision by the court through objection and appeal. I hope I have been able to rectify that omission herewith. I then have another question from Ms. Wingelaar and Mr. Boutkan. How many decisions are taken under the LAP, how many copies are expected for processing, and how much time and resources does the answering cost? I just briefly mentioned that as well. The number of decisions taken is unknown. That is ultimately up to the competent authorities. The proposal concerns deviations from the rules in the CMP. It therefore does not concern every permit that is issued; it really concerns the deviations. That obligation already exists and the procedure remains virtually the same. It is therefore not about something new. Following the CMP is therefore the norm, as you also just said. Deviating is therefore the exception. That is why we expect few notifications and limited costs. How much time the assessment requires ultimately depends on the reported deviation itself. Incidentally, the competent authority does not have to wait for an answer. Given the information we have, we therefore expect it to be a limited number of cases. However, I must also be very honest and say that it is not always reported to us. That makes it difficult for us to keep track of the situation.

Certainly. I have always acted in good faith. I will undoubtedly have made a mistake now and then; I admit that. But I have always gone all out. That applies to private equity as well. I think the entire House agrees with me that we want to tackle the profiteers, the people dipping their fingers into the healthcare coffers. And that is what we are going to do. We are going to do this with the Act on the Ethical Operation of Healthcare Institutions and Youth Institutions. For everything I have said, I have always sought evidence. I think I have achieved a great deal by removing those components from market forces and through the agreements we are going to make. I have done my utmost to realize my ideals. Does this mean that I will succeed at everything? No, of course not. I also sat in on all those legislative processes for many years and, regardless of the image you have, I was also very constructive regarding legislation.

The documents we received from the Ministry of the Interior are crystal clear. There is no way to dispute them. Impossible, undesirable, undemocratic. If the Minister for Asylum and Migration says that her best legal experts have also reviewed them, but that they have been completely redacted, then that is truly remarkable. The House should say: we want those documents too, and not in the way we have received them now, namely page after page redacted. Otherwise, there is only one story, and that is the story we have from the Ministry of the Interior. That is that this is absolutely unacceptable.

That is really too easy for me. The PVV says "we had to give money to Ukraine," but a gigantic sum is being given to it: 7.3 billion euros, more than the entire social welfare system. You would expect a cabinet led by the PVV—for you are also opposed to that support, or you are critical of it, just like us—to at least keep it within limits and not spend such a gigantic amount on it. Didn't you allow yourself to be completely outmaneuvered at that table?

These administrators are unleashing a revolution. They are my healthcare heroes. They were asked the question — as you can also see in the broadcast — whether they, like Ms. Agema, thought that administrative time would be halved due to AI. That is untrue. That question was put to them. As a result, they were misled. You could see in the coalition program that, following artificial intelligence, there is a huge list of measures we are taking to halve administrative time. So that was truly unjustified.
The motion on document no. 992, which is also from the PVV, concerns the sniffer dogs. You spoke of sixteen motions, Chair, but I have fifteen.

… and as hateful. Those kinds of words are all being used. We say that this is a very unfair narrative: if people are crossing all those safe countries, we shouldn't act as if the Netherlands is the world's saving angel. That is simply not true. People are making a deliberate attempt to reach the Netherlands — the worst part is that this isn't being slowed down either, so people take that little boat across the Mediterranean — even though they already had safety. It is therefore not at all strange that politicians in the Netherlands say: no, sorry, you should have stayed in the first European country where you were taken under protection. Look, if we all undermine that rule, it’s the end of the line.
The Cabinet believes that the parties must abide by the ceasefire, which was reached under great pressure. That is the Cabinet's position.

Thank you for that, but the problem with migration is precisely that it happens to us. It has been happening to us for twenty years. So you actually need those goals to get the entire apparatus on board with the objective that migration must decrease. By that, I mean not just the civil servants, but also the COA, the IND, and the entire chain. The objective is that migration must decrease. You see this in countries that are successful in migration policy, in slowing down migration. They have all set very clear targets and know what they are doing. Those organizations are completely permeated with this: that is how we will bring it down. Otherwise, it remains just patching things up and muddling through, and above all: sink or swim. We really need to get rid of that.

We would like to see an availability fund established to help hospitals in trouble. It must meet a great many strict conditions. It is not a case of handing over a pot of money and letting the hospital do as it pleases. With that fund, we want to ensure continuity for hospital care …

I am quite willing to send a letter. I am trying to do so before the May recess. But I can already lift a corner of the veil: it is going with difficulty. That applies, incidentally, to all the discussions we are currently holding. Mr. Chairman, I will continue. I have one more question from Mr. Stoffer regarding my introduction. Cuts are being made, and it is difficult for provinces to keep up. How am I going to implement the tasks set out in the Framework Agreement? How am I going to improve the accessibility of rural areas and strengthen bus transport between village centers? The reduction in the BDU is truly an incredibly high priority for me. We have already discussed this regularly with the House. I am also working—as I just indicated during the two-minute debate—to ensure, with a view to the Spring Memorandum, that the funding for public transport is cut back as little as possible. We are discussing this with the transport operators, the decentralized authorities, and within the Cabinet. In the letter I sent to the House, you could see that we asked for clarification regarding what this might entail. Naturally, we were all very shocked by this. That is something you do not want. I already stated in the last debate that I believe it could have disastrous consequences if we cannot rectify the developments surrounding the BDU. I still stand by those words. Now, I turn to the accessibility folder, Chair. First, the question from Mr. De Hoop of GroenLinks-Labour Party. Article 1 of the Constitution has been amended by the addition of discrimination on the grounds of disability. What am I going to do to ensure that it is more accessible by 2040? The ambition is to make public transport fully accessible to everyone by 2040. That ambition was also expressed by Mr. De Hoop. The Public Transport Accessibility Administrative Agreement makes a significant contribution to this ambition. I think it is also important to mention that we have been working on improving accessibility since 2006, including through ProRail’s Train Station Accessibility Program. Currently, over 90% of journeys are made to an accessible station, all NS Sprinters are equipped with low-floor access, and travel assistance is scheduled to be available at all train stations by 2025. So, we have been working on this for quite some time and have already achieved a great deal. I can also add the following. At one point during the first term, I was asked: what is being done and how are experts by experience being involved in this? Last Thursday, I had an administrative meeting with the NOVB, the National Public Transport Council. There, I once again drew attention to the accessibility of public transport. I also had contact with the State Secretary for Health, Welfare and Sport the day before. We asked the sector to share good experiences with one another and to include experts by experience in this process. The response from the administrative meeting was very positive. A number of examples were also presented. Provinces came up with ideas regarding national cooperation on travel assistance. This theme is already being addressed in a special working group within the NOVB. The province of South Holland has dual-mode ramps for wheelchairs, which can be unfolded both mechanically and manually. Another example was that information displays in buses are positioned so that they are visible even when seated in a wheelchair facing backwards rather than forwards. The Amsterdam Transport Region collaborates with the Cliëntenbelang foundation, which is also actively involved in the design and procurement of bus and tram stops. NS also cited the practical example that lift malfunctions will be included in the journey planner, so that these can be taken into account when planning a trip. As far as I am concerned, these are all excellent examples being exchanged. This gives me confidence that we all indeed view accessibility as a top priority, including within the sector, and that we are working daily to improve it even further. I therefore believe these are positive developments.

I disagree with that. A political signal has been sent. I also understand that an opt-out is a long-term process, but I will continue to push for it because it strengthens the signal. The support has clearly been received. The message that we want a stricter European migration policy has also been well received. Things simply have to change; there is simply a different wind blowing in Europe. Again, I haven't been rejected; the message came across well, and there is a conversation, a discussion, going on within Europe because we are seriously considering this. Yes, of course there are countries that are somewhat less enthusiastic, but there are also countries that are following us very seriously in this matter. So I think the message has been received well. The discussion has been sparked, and that is always a good thing.

I am not screaming. I am holding you accountable for your own words. Everything you just said was explained, in whole or in part, during the hearings with the ministers when they were still candidates. It was placed in context there, including the examples you just mentioned, such as Ms. Klever speaking about the diseases you mention and the coronavirus that was prevalent at the time. I am not going to repeat all of that. But it is very reprehensible that you and your party are calling our people to account for something, while we say: "We shouldn't have done it that way and we didn't mean that context." We will discuss our major concerns regarding the effects on the Netherlands — and this is, once again, shared by millions of people — of immigration and asylum. That is what we will discuss, and not that terrible theory anymore. No one, not a single PVV member in the House of Representatives, in the cabinet, or in the Netherlands, has any affinity with racist Nazi theories. Here in this House, we are just about the greatest friend of the Jewish people and the State of Israel. How dare you pin that on us? But the worst part is that the very moment we have said something about this, for political reasons — and purely and solely for political reasons — you keep the knife on the table and say to those people: "You are no good." By doing so, you are doing those people an injustice. By doing so, you are destroying families. You are destroying those people completely, because you are simply saying: "There are half-Nazis in the room; there are racists in the room." That is what you are saying! If you are a man, then you will submit motions of no confidence right now, and we are going to vote on them *now*, Mr. Chairman, because I cannot leave that hanging over the House and over the table. You call none of us that, or you are going to attach political consequences to that *now*! The fact that you are doing this while people have retracted statements and said they will not make those statements again means that you are a politician who is not worth a dime. I stand up for our government ministers with pride.

Well, I'm absolutely flabbergasted! I referred to the IPCC report. The Minister is now dismissing that with: well, that's just a little report... We've been discussing that the whole time in the House, that institute and that report. That is what we referred to. And those authors themselves say, on the page numbers I mentioned — I can read out the quotes — that this is the case, that there is no trend towards more hurricanes, that there is no more drought. They say that themselves. That is the main report that everyone has always referred to. In addition, there are many other scientists who say the same thing, for example, the 2,000 scientists who signed that climate declaration, including Nobel laureates. And I could go on like this for a while. I also showed an article based on satellite data. This Minister now says, "no, I am not going to doubt it, *the* science says that this is the case," but it simply isn't. It *is* not the case. Is it not then very irresponsible — and that is now my question to the Minister, through you, Chair — for a Minister to say, "I am not going to think about it, I know exactly how things stand, I have no doubts"? Surely that is not an attitude?

For some subjects, yes, and for others, no. In Hamburg, you have HafenCity. There is a container terminal there. There is constant noise from containers, trains, cranes, and who knows what else. But people live there too. They have signed a contract agreeing to accept this and stating that they will not litigate. They have a magnificent view of the harbor. A water person like me loves that. So do those people. Why couldn't what is possible in Hamburg be possible here too? Why don't we arrange it the same way here? That does require us to look at things differently. We have made air quality part of the Environment and Planning Act. In other countries, they have done it differently. The standards still apply, but you can agree among yourselves to temporarily remove them from the environmental impact assessment. That standard still remains in place, because the importance of clean air lies behind it, but you aren't going to arrange that in an afternoon. I am really looking in all sorts of ways to see if I can organize this differently. But if I don't have your support, I simply won't succeed.

Listen. What tremendous arrogance it is that you accuse me of having no moral compass! That does not do you credit. That is arrogant. And I do not accept that from you either. My moral compass… I am no less or more than you. It is truly very wrong merely to suggest this. Once again, there was the little interruption debate earlier: it has been politicized and the minister is allowed to do that. There are rules—a colleague already referred to them—stating: if a minister decides not to sign an application for such a medal, then it goes to the Council of Ministers. That rule would not have existed if the legislator—it concerned rules of procedure at the time—had not provided for it. But it was also an issue earlier, even back in the day. I looked it up again. It concerns the CDA/VVD Van Agt I cabinet, the cabinet of which your kindred spirits were a part, as you know. I have a document in front of me about Nazi hunter Simon Wiesenthal. People might still remember that. He was to receive an award in the Netherlands in 1978. That was refused at the time by CDA/ARP Minister Job de Ruiter, because they felt there was too much criticism from Wiesenthal regarding how the Menten case had been handled. Six months later, in March 1979, he received that award after all. So it is politicized. The colleague from Forum for Democracy said it rightly, I think: the fact that you entrust that authority to a minister makes it political. In the past, too, it has been rejected and politicized for very different reasons and also by CDA ministers. If you don't want that, then you shouldn't leave it to politics. So it is not amoral. It is just how the system works.

I wasn't finished yet. Excuse me, Prime Minister. Who is going to pay for that? I can fill that in: everyone is going to pay for it, not the wealthy. That is a political choice. There is nothing pragmatic about it. It is a deeply ideological choice to make people pay for it again, through VAT increases, through austerity measures, and through the rising national debt. Once again, the people with the largest fortunes, Box 3 — it is the very highest incomes and the very highest assets that are taxed there — get off lightly. No, it is once again not levied on the people who have assets. Where is it being taken from? From ordinary people. "Passionate pragmatism" is the preservation of the status quo. It is pure, deeply neoliberal to act as if neoliberalism is bankrupt and no longer exists. In all your statements to date — I have just mentioned two — you are deeply, deeply neoliberal. Now, however, a kind of veneer is being applied as if you were an outside prime minister, non-partisan. I would very much like a slightly deeper analysis from this Prime Minister than "passionate pragmatism". What is your view of humanity? What are your values? What will you be working on with this country in the coming years?

I was waiting for the bridge, but this was the bridge the SP was going to build. It is not without reason that our parliamentary group leader, Dilan Yeşilgöz, indicated during last week's debate: let us start working on the emergency bill in parallel, alongside drafting that emergency bill, because there is a risk that the emergency bill will not make it. Our ministers also wrote this in previous letters when they were still ministers. There is a risk. Suppose the emergency bill fails — we are not aiming for that, but suppose it happens — then we want to prevent losing time. That is the route we indicated last week, and nothing has changed regarding that.
The last point. This cabinet is fully committed to return. That is truly our priority, and not the remigration of people who are integrated into Dutch society.

That is also completely untrue. We want to tackle asylum. Asylum seekers cost more and stay longer than labor migrants. But we also want to tackle labor migration. Of course we also want to tackle labor migration. We also want to tackle student migration. Name one migration that we do not want to tackle. I challenge you.

Ms. Kamminga says very convincingly: if it cannot be done within the EU, where can it? Well, it turns out it cannot be done within the EU either, because France is supplying those weapons. Those weapons end up in Sudan and God knows what other conflict zones. Because those weapons are exported to places where there is war, and therefore also to Sudan. So that goes via France. There is ample evidence that this is happening, and yet Ms. Kamminga says: go ahead and join that treaty, do not wait, do it as soon as possible. And that while she knows that these weapons end up there. How important does the VVD still consider the arms export criteria then?

At the moment, no deliveries are taking place, so… No deliveries are being made, so there is no question of human rights violations occurring with Dutch equipment. And what is happening in the Middle East is terrible. That is truly terrible. We are also pushing for a very quick ceasefire. The diplomatic effort is focused on that, because absolutely no one wants this. There simply aren't any… No weapons are being delivered to Israel. Human rights violations are also taken into account in the considerations for the permits, but there are no permits involved at the moment.

Excuse me, but I have a question regarding the motion on document no. 46, because I do not understand it. This motion requests the government not to proceed with reparations for the transatlantic slave trade. The Minister gives the motion a judgment from the House. But was this actually on the agenda? Was the Cabinet actually planning to proceed with reparations?

I hear the request, but there are effectively only three working days left before many go on recess and leave. I therefore ask for real leniency in this regard. I will try to expedite as much as possible. There were three brief questions remaining, and then I will move on to the motions. Mr. Paternotte asked: why is the Netherlands failing to meet the implementation date for the NIS2 Directive? The Minister of Justice and Security stated on October 26 that the delay is due to the fact that transposing national legislation is a large and complex process. In the meantime, two legislative proposals were sent to the Council of State on December 2. Depending on the time required for that advice, the proposals are expected to be sent to your House in the first quarter of 2025. No fewer than 150 responses were received, so it took some time to process them. Mr. Van Campen also inquired about the Coast Guard. With the growth of sanctions against Russia, national enforcement capacity has been expanded in recent years. However, that capacity is under pressure. The Cabinet considers it important that capacity keeps pace with the expansion of sanctions; See also the letter from the Minister of Foreign Affairs dated 22 November last. Incidentally, for the measures in the eleventh package, the tasks have been assigned to the Human Environment and Transport Inspectorate and the Ministry of Infrastructure and Water Management. The Coast Guard plays an important role on other subjects. Then, one more question from Ms. Teunissen regarding Mercosur. Your House will in any case be involved in the decision-making regarding the Dutch position in the Council. Without a decision by the Council, no trade agreement can enter into force. In addition to and on top of that, in accordance with your motion of 2023, the Netherlands is aiming for the Commission to submit the treaty as an integral association agreement, which means that the Netherlands itself must become a party to the treaty and would have to ratify that treaty. That cannot be done without your House.

That is also the starting point. As I just said: only in cases of very serious matters—statements such as "I am going to commit suicide," "I am going to commit murder," or "I am going to carry out a terrorist attack"—does the GDPR allow scope to overrule. The information discussed in a conversation is pseudonymized in any case. It is pseudonymized in any case when it involves peer supervision or mapping trends. All of this takes place without identifiable personal data.

But it is "predictable" that this should not be the answer, because it is not code black; it is code pitch black for the rule of law. Victims and surviving relatives call me and say: Eerdmans, do something about this! Under a cabinet including the PVV, prisoners are being released or sent home with an ankle monitor. Sorry, but there must be an answer to that. I do find it truly remarkable that if we adopt two motions here… We do not want to follow England’s example and avoid British conditions in which we release thousands of criminals early, but we are going to do it anyway. I have an adopted motion here, submitted with Ms. Bruyning of NSC, regarding no detention holidays for criminals, yet it is happening nonetheless. Chair. What is this State Secretary going to focus on then, for example in the Spring Budget Memorandum? There is 8 billion left over, I understand from a reliable source, namely the CPB. That concerns underspending. Is this State Secretary then going to say in the Spring Budget Memorandum: I want money reserved for emergency prisons; I want money set aside for that FAST team and I want to arrest more fugitive criminals, because I am from the PVV and I want to do things differently? You are copying the Weerwind method. I mean, the previous minister was even stricter than the State Secretary from the PVV, and just for the listeners: he was from D66. Mr. Chairman. I simply want an answer to that.

Yes, also from Mr. Van Nispen of the SP. I advise against it. The request in the motion to establish a single central point of contact has already been largely fulfilled by designating Helpwanted as an accessible reporting facility. Helpwanted itself offers assistance to victims, including the removal of unlawful and criminal content, or it hands the case over to the police or specialist assistance. In this regard, Helpwanted acts as a gateway for victims. We are currently examining how the processing of a request for assistance can be optimized within Helpwanted.

Women's shelters are actually the policy domain of the State Secretary for Long-Term and Social Care, my colleague. She is already holding administrative discussions with the VNG and Valente with the aim of securing sufficient shelter places. But I leave it up to the House.

In previous lives, I have traveled the whole world and seen many countries, countries that are corrupt and countries that are not. I also received a lot of criticism when I visited certain countries: "Oh, there he comes again from the Netherlands, thinking he knows it all better." That doesn't bother me. Because if we keep having those discussions, none of us will benefit from it. What matters is that we start conducting discussions based on facts. Hence Transparency International. We are indeed simply continuing with our current activities this year, including in the area of integrity, but I want to conduct that discussion differently next year. I am indifferent to what people think of me. That is not why I was hired. I was hired here to achieve results in the areas of good governance, sound finances—including tax collection—that is another one—and self-reliance, economic development, so that the people there—because it is not about me, but about the people there—will be better off in a few years' time than they are now.

There is something else very strange, because we very often prescribe that something is not allowed, but meanwhile... That was also the case with electric collars and certain choke chains. At a certain point, they were no longer allowed to be used, but they were still allowed to be sold. I find that typical of the Netherlands. I find that unbelievable. We say that certain dogs may no longer be bred here, but you can bring them here from abroad and keep them here. So there must also be a ban on keeping them, naturally with a phase-out clause. So if people already have such an animal, then of course it may stay with those people until its death. Everyone should understand what I mean. Now comes the stick behind the door: the wolf, the most important predator in the Netherlands. Perhaps it is, together with the fox. Regarding the wolf, I also mentioned a veterinarian from Gilze en Rijen who has a son living in Drenthe. He sent me a lot of photos. He is a veterinarian who is no longer active. So you see a wolf walking with a mole in its mouth, with all sorts of things. So, the stories that a wolf only preys on sheep are all nonsense. A wolf prefers to eat any small animals it encounters in areas preferably far away from humans. But the moment humans start excessively shooting roe deer and wild boars, a wolf is often forced to seek its food elsewhere, especially if it has cubs. That is a system of communicating vessels. This is my opinion on the matter. Who decides that we decide which animal is allowed to live or what that animal is allowed to eat? None of us have a say in that: not the Minister, not the State Secretary, not the 150 Members of Parliament, and not the citizens. That is not our decision. Those are the facts, and we must leave it at that. The House, having heard the deliberations, requests the government, should it decide to proceed with controlling our wolf packs, to make the deployment of contraceptive doctors possible, and proceeds to the order of the day.

So policy is needed. And your proposal is therefore not enough. So you are abandoning the Dutch people by making the promise: if we do this, it will be fixed. Now we are discovering that it is not fixed with just the PVV promises. That is why my question, which I am also putting to you…

Thank you, Chair. Thank you to Ms. Koekkoek of Volt for putting this topic on the agenda and for the question she is asking. What am I going to do about this? I can say the following regarding that. The Cabinet acknowledges that the change to the proposed reduction in motor vehicle tax rates compared to the Spring Memorandum does not contribute to the predictability of policy for citizens and businesses, nor to reliable governance. At the same time, the Cabinet is also responsible for ensuring that public finances remain in order. This is also part of good and reliable governance. The objective of sustainability is important, but it must be weighed against feasibility within the budget. The Cabinet does not consider it desirable to resolve this by increasing the burden on other citizens and businesses and has therefore chosen to reduce the rate reduction. Although this will be lower than previously announced, it still partially compensates for the technical additional weight of a zero-emission passenger car. In the Spring Memorandum of 2025 — that is my answer to Ms. Koekkoek — the level of the rate reduction will be re-evaluated by the Cabinet. Until then, I will have to leave it at that.

I can elaborate further. It concerns the people who have applied for asylum here in the Netherlands. That is what I am focusing on initially. You might not consider that an answer, Mr. Van Nispen, but I do consider it an answer. It concerns the influx of asylum seekers. These people have not applied for asylum in the Netherlands. I understand very well what this is about.
Mr. Sneller may conclude what he wants. I have said what I have said.

I am, of course, always in dialogue with Schiphol, and we certainly pay attention to tariffs and to investments that lead to higher rates, but ultimately, airlines determine their own rates. Look, the tax I have to introduce comes from the coalition agreement. There is nothing I can do about that. The rates naturally depend on many factors, including the market price of crude oil and, of course, the price of kerosene. So I understand the concerns, but I have limited options for action. The European Commission can investigate whether tariffs violate European competition law. The Authority for Consumers and Markets can also conduct an investigation, but otherwise, it is really the airlines and the airport themselves that determine their rates.

No, that is not what I said. We have already had an interruption debate about that several times. What I have said several times, also directed at Mr. Krul, is the following. The hospital's decision regarding the structure from 2030 onwards was made before the coalition program was presented. With the coalition program, we are going to reorganize the healthcare landscape: from concentration to dispersion. We are creating a different landscape than the one that exists now. We are creating a landscape in which regional hospitals and small hospitals in the big city gain a right to exist and a different image emerges around them. After all, they have been shaky and uncertain for a very long time. We are ensuring that, even when it comes to South Limburg, there can be more reasons for doctors and nurses throughout the country to think: perhaps I would really like to settle in that beautiful mining region. That is the small loophole I see for the decision the hospital has already made. Regarding that, I say: you have already made a decision, but please, give me another chance to discuss this further. Maybe they will make a different decision then. Surely you can still make a different decision after you have made one? But I did say that I am not going to give false hope. If this is it, then this is it. Then I have done my utmost and pulled out all the stops.

I was only halfway through. Perhaps it is good to say this again, but we have already had a debate about this. I was questioned then because I was standing here as a PVV member to explain what we thought of the report. I addressed that. I thought this debate was intended to respond to the cabinet's letter on this matter, but I am happy to explain once again what the PVV considers important. I have done that to some extent. As far as we are concerned, there is no need for additional funding for legal aid. The right to contact is a nice thing, and one you would want, but if you start defining that as a right, we paint ourselves into a corner. Because if someone happens to be unable to make contact because they are busy or for whatever reason, we end up with fines and that kind of misery again. I always keep in mind... That is a whole other story. I also got to debate nature for a while. Then, at a certain point, it turned to water quality. Everyone here always says that the water quality is much worse now. That is simply not the case. But what is the case? A few years ago, we agreed that water quality should roughly meet the standards of a Swiss mountain stream. We cannot achieve that. So, I have debates here with people who keep saying: "Yes, but it doesn't meet this and it doesn't meet that, and that is bad." But it is a fact that the water in the Netherlands is much better than it was 20 to 30 years ago. I am allowed to say that, because I am that old. In those days, you were allowed or not allowed to swim in some lakes. I say that as an example to illustrate this, Mr. Chairman. It is a bit of a long introduction, but I am an old man, and that is what happens at some point. To align myself with Mr. Sneller's youthful enthusiasm... If you just promise all sorts of things regarding those recommendations, in a few months we will be having debates again about promises we cannot keep. So, you have to be careful. You have to look at each recommendation and each conclusion to see if it is possible at all, and whether it is also possible in the short term. That is why we have a cabinet that is responding and introducing laws that we will consider one by one. But to simply say "we are adopting nineteen recommendations" without being able to predict — and that applies to you as well, Mr. Sneller — whether it can all be done within a few months, I do not think is right or justified.

The PVV is responsible for the Ministry of Infrastructure and Water Management. The PVV is the largest party, so the PVV is responsible for what is happening now. That is why I ask the question once again: will the PVV accept that public transport — which they have already allowed to become more expensive; under this State Secretary, train fares were already increased by 6% — will become even more expensive? And the second question is the following: Will the PVV accept that tram and bus lines will disappear, so that people in the region will soon have even less public transport available?

I find it difficult, because I am a strong advocate of saving on healthcare by improving it. But if you look at the cuts and savings in healthcare — because there are cuts included as well — that list has become very long by now. It is also up to all of us to realize all of that. I struggle with the fact that healthcare is always singled out for even more savings on top of that whole list that is already there and that we are simply expected to implement.

Well, we don't want to wait long for Europe; we are currently working within the European Union to achieve a PFAS ban. Until then, we naturally have a specific policy in place, and we assess permits against that policy. There is not yet a ban on the discharge of PFAS in the regulations, so in that sense, I cannot currently refuse permits that fall within the scope provided by the law. We are choosing to achieve a European ban rather than a Dutch one, especially because you frequently see that what is discharged in Belgium and Germany, or further upstream, ultimately ends up in the Netherlands anyway. It is, of course, by far the best approach to address this at the European level.

You will have to continue this debate with the Minister of Social Affairs and Employment. What you say about the trial balloon is incorrect. A new scheme is necessary in any case, because the current SOV expires in March 2027. I also no longer want a subsidy scheme; I want a statutory regulation. I want to arrange things properly for these people. That is my intention. Everyone is free to doubt that, but it is indeed my intention to make things better.

I fully believe that the cabinet acted sensibly in the phasing out process, but wasn't it the case that that 1.5 billion was primarily needed for Box 3 problems? I understood that correctly, didn't I? That was also the accompanying message. So, in other words: if those problems hadn't existed, the phasing out would still have been sensible, but more money would have gone to middle incomes. That is how it is, isn't it?

Yes. We are certainly actively working on that. For example, early next year we will be holding roundtable discussions with the sector, regulators, and consumer advocacy groups to gather all those signals. The ACM and the Dutch Data Protection Authority are already warning in campaigns: know what you are agreeing to. The solution naturally also lies partly at the European level with the Digital Services Act. An investigation is currently underway there. The European Commission is already conducting actual investigations. To reassure Mr. Krul, I believe I just made a fairly strong statement to consumers on behalf of the Cabinet for the first time, urging them to think very carefully before making use of this. This also relates to so-called dark patterns, through which you are lured via countdown timers and similar algorithms. We endorse the concern. That is also how I began my response to Member Krul. I am pleased that he is drawing attention to this from the House, because it is a legitimate concern. We endorse it. Together with my fellow ministers, we are in contact regarding this. In some previous debates, we have also addressed this in more detail. Thank you for the signal. I take that into account wholeheartedly. Succession is in place. It is already receiving a great deal of attention. With the statement I have just made, I hope to reassure the House that this has our full attention and is a major priority.

I have a question for Mr. Bamenga of D66. It concerns the delivery of humanitarian aid. Israel has provided more than 1.7 million tons of food and humanitarian aid through COGAT, a subsidiary of the IDF. So, I understand from Mr. Bamenga that he wants to exclude this aid completely? Does that mean he wants to completely exclude all aid that COGAT has provided to the Palestinians?
